questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Indian Statutory Commission, 1927:
I. 	 It was appointed according to the provision of
the Government of India Act, 1919.
II. 	 It was supported by the liberals of the Hindu
Mahasabha and the Muslim League.
III. 	It established the Communal Award that set
up separate electorates for minorities.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Simon Commission:** The **Government of India Act, 1919 had a provision** that a commission would be **appointed ten years from the date** to study the progress of the governance scheme and suggest new steps.  An **all-white, seven-member Indian Statutory Commission**, popularly known as the **Simon** **Commission** (after the name of its chairman, Sir John Simon), was set up by the British Government under Stanley Baldwin’s Prime Ministership on **November 8, 1927. So, statement I is correct.**
○ The commission was to **recommend to the British Government** whether India was ready for further constitutional reforms and along what lines. ○ The Congress session in Madras (December 1927) meeting under the presidency of M.A. Ansari decided to boycott the commission **“at every stage and in every form”.** Those who decided to support the Congress call of boycott of the Simon Commission included the liberals of the **Hindu** **Mahasabha and the majority faction of the Muslim League under Muhammad Ali Jinnah**. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Communal award:**  The Communal Award was announced by the **British Prime Minister, Ramsay** **MacDonald**, on **August 16, 1932.**
○ The Communal Award, based on the findings of the **Indian Franchise Committee (also called** **the Lothian Committee),** established separate electorates and reserved seats for minorities, including the depressed classes which were granted seventy-eight reserved seats. **So, statement** **III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The recommendation of the President is
required for introducing a bill to form a new
State in India.
II. 	 An Indian territory can be ceded to a foreign
country through ordinary legislation in
Parliament.
III. 	A State can change its name anytime by
passing a motion with special majority in the
State Legislative Assembly.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 3 of the Indian Constitution** authorises the Parliament to:
○ f**orm a new State** by separation of territory from any state or by uniting two or more States or parts of States or by uniting any territory to a part of any State ○ increase the area of any State ○ diminish the area of any State ○ alter the boundaries of any State ○ **alter the name of any State**
● Article 3 lays down that, a bill contemplating the above changes can be introduced in the Parliament **only** **with the prior recommendation of the President**; and before recommending the bill; the President has to refer the same to the State Legislature concerned for expressing its views within a specified period. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The President (or Parliament) is not bound by the views of the state legislature and may either accept or reject them, even if the views are received in time.
● In Berubari case (1960), the Supreme Court held that the power of Parliament to diminish the area of a state (under Article 3) does not cover cession of Indian Territory to a foreign country. Hence, **Indian** **Territory can be ceded to a foreign state** **only by amending the Constitution** under Article 368. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● The Parliament has the final authority to change the name of the State and not the State Legislatures. In case of renaming a state, a State Legislature needs to pass a resolution for the same, which is then sent to the Centre. The Union cabinet then decides to approve or reject the Constitution Amendment Bill, since it requires amending the Constitution, which both Houses of Parliament need to pass by a simple majority. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● The Constitution authorises the Parliament to form new States or alter the areas, boundaries or names of the existing States **without their consent**. In other words, the Parliament can redraw the political map of India according to its will. Hence, the territorial integrity or continued existence of any state is not guaranteed by the Constitution. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following audits is/are obligatory on
the part of the Comptroller and Auditor General
(CAG) of India?
I. 	 Legal and regulatory audit to ensure
expenditure conforms to the governing
authority
II. 	 Propriety audit to look into the wisdom and
economy of government expenditure
III. 	Performance audit to assess effectiveness in
the application of public funds
IV. 	 Audit of secret service expenditure to verify
the detailed particulars of the incurred costs
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG)** of India derives his/her auditing powers from the **Constitution** and the **CAG’s (Duties, Powers and Conditions of Service) Act, 1971.** While the scope of audit has expanded over time, there is a clear distinction between mandatory (obligatory) and discretionary functions.
● The CAG is mandated to ascertain whether money disbursed were legally available and whether the expenditure conforms to the authority that governs it. This **legal and regulatory audit** is specifically described as **obligatory** on the part of the CAG. **So, point I is correct.**
● The CAG can conduct a **propriety audit** to comment on the "wisdom, faithfulness, and economy" of expenditure. However, the text clarifies that, unlike the legal audit, the propriety audit is **discretionary**. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Performance audit** is concerned with the audit of economy, efficiency, and effectiveness. While the Ministry of Finance clarified in 2006 that this falls within the CAG's scope, it is a comprehensive appraisal of progress and efficiency rather than a statutory obligation like the regulatory audit. **So, point III is not** **correct.**
● In the case of **secret service expenditure**, the CAG has a significant limitation. He/she **cannot call for** **particulars** of the expenditure and must accept a certificate from the competent administrative authority. Therefore, a detailed audit of these costs is not possible, let alone obligatory. **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the office of the Cabinet Secretary
in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Cabinet Secretary is directly responsible
to the Prime Minister and reports him
concerning all administrative matters.
II. 	 The Cabinet Secretary serves as the ex-officio
chairperson of the Civil Services Board.
III. 	The Constitution of India provides a fixed
tenure of five years to the Cabinet Secretary
to ensure administrative continuity.
How many of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Cabinet Secretary** is the highest-ranking civil servant in the Government of India and serves as the head of the Cabinet Secretariat. This office acts as a vital link between the political executive and the permanent civil service, ensuring effective coordination across various departments.
● As a matter of convention, the senior-most civil servant belonging to the **Indian Administrative Service** **(IAS)** is appointed to this post. The appointment is made based on special qualities like tact, energy, and efficiency to command the respect of all department heads. The Cabinet Secretary is **directly responsible** **to the Prime Ministe**r (PM) and reports to the PM concerning all matters. **So**, **statement I is correct.**
● The Cabinet Secretary holds several ex-officio positions, including being the **chairperson of the Civil** **Services Board** and the head of the Indian Administrative Services. He/she also chairs the Committee of Secretaries on administration. **So**, **statement II is correct.**
● There is **no fixed tenure** prescribed by the Constitution for the Cabinet Secretary. Generally, the incumbent has a tenure of 2 to 3 years, and while the average tenure does not exceed 3 years, the government has the power to extend it. **So**, **statement III is not correct.** **So, only two of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The **Gopal Swamy Ayyangar Report (1949)** was instrumental in defining the role of the Cabinet Secretary. It recommended that the Secretary be regarded as the "first member of the public services," ensuring that the government and the services could rely on his/her impartiality.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Fundamental Rights
enshrined in the Constitution of India, which of
the following provisions have direct horizontal
application?
I. 	 Prohibition of discrimination in access
to shops, hotels, and places of public
entertainment
II. 	 Protection against the retrospective
application of criminal laws
III. 	Abolition of untouchability in any form
IV. 	 Prohibition of human trafficking and forced
labor
V. 	 Freedom to manage religious affairs
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:

options.b: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**: The horizontal application of Fundamental Rights implies that **private individuals** and entities can be held accountable for violating constitutional rights. While most rights are vertical (enforceable only against the State), the Indian Constitution contains specific provisions designed to transform social relations by binding **non-state** **actors as well.**
● **Article 15 (2)** of the Constitution of India explicitly states that no citizen shall be subjected to restrictions in access to **shops, public restaurants, hotels, and places of public entertainment**. This applies horizontally, meaning private owners are bound by this non-discrimination clause. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Article 20 (1)** of the Constitution of India provides protection against **ex-post-facto laws** (retrospective criminal laws). This is a classic vertical right intended to restrict the power of the **Legislature** from passing such laws and the **Executive** from enforcing them. It **does not** govern the relationship between two private individuals. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Article 17** of the Constitution of India prohibits **untouchability** in any form. It is enforceable against both the State and private individuals. Any act of untouchability is a punishable offence, making it a clear case of direct horizontal application to end social exclusion. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Article 23** of the Constitution of India prohibits **human trafficking and forced labor** (begar). This provision is enforceable against **private individuals** and groups, ensuring that citizens are protected from exploitation by non-state players such as contractors or private employers. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Article 26** (Freedom to manage religious affairs) is a right granted to religious denominations. While it protects them, it is primarily a right used **against the State** to prevent interference in religious matters, **rather than a horizontal right** used by one individual against another private actor. **So, point V is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indian parliamentary system,
consider the following:
I. 	 Impeachment of the President for the violation
of the Constitution
II. 	 Removal of the Vice-President from office
III. 	Removal of the judges of the Supreme Court
of India
IV. 	 Advising to the President for the removal of
the Comptroller and Auditor General
How many of the above judicial functions are
performed by the Parliament of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Parliament of India exercises certain judicial powers and functions that allow it to act as a quasi-judicial body in specific circumstances. These powers are primarily designed to ensure constitutional accountability of highranking officials and to protect the dignity and privileges of the legislative house.
● The Parliament can **impeach the President** specifically for the "violation of the Constitution" under the procedure laid down in Article 61. **So, point I is correct.**
● It has the power to **remove the Vice-President** from office; this process involves a resolution passed by the Rajya Sabha and agreed to by the Lok Sabha. **So, point II is correct.**
● The Parliament **recommends the removal** of the Chief Justice and judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts to the President after an address is passed by both Houses. The final order of **removal is issued by** **the** **President**. **So, point III is correct.**
● It is empowered to **recommend the removal** of high constitutional authorities, like **Comptroller and** **Auditor General (CAG)**, to the President. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, all four of the above judicial functions are performed by the Parliament of India.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to Anti-defection Law:
I. 	 The President is the authority who takes final decisions on all anti-defection cases.
II. 	 Defying party whip in voting when asked by the party leadership is deemed as defection.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● To restrict a legislator who is elected on one party’s ticket from ‘defecting’ to another Party, an amendment to the Constitution was made i.e., 52nd Amendment Act in 1985. This is known as anti-defection amendment. It has also been subsequently modified by the 91st Amendment of 2003.
● The presiding officer of the House is the authority who takes final decisions on all such cases. If it is proved that a member has ‘defected’, then such member loses the membership of the House. Besides, such a person is also disqualified from holding any political office like ministership, etc. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● If a member remains absent in the House when asked by the party leadership to remain present or votes against the instructions of the party or voluntarily leaves the membership of the party, it is deemed as defection. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
As per the 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992, reservation of seats in Panchayati Raj elections is mandatory for which of the following categories?
I. 	 Scheduled Castes
II. 	 Scheduled Tribes
III. 	Other Backward Classes
IV. 	 Women
V. 	 Persons with Disabilities
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992,** inserted **Part IX** in the Constitution of India, providing a threetier structure of Panchayati Raj. To ensure the participation of marginalized and underrepresented sections of society, the Act includes specific provisions for the reservation of seats and offices of chairpersons.
● The Act provides for the reservation of seats for **Scheduled Castes (SCs)** and **Scheduled Tribes (STs)** in every panchayat at all three levels. The number of seats reserved is in proportion to their population to the total population in the panchayat area. **So, points I and II are correct.**
● Regarding **Other Backward Classes (OBCs)**, the 73rd Amendment Act does not mandate reservation. Instead, it **authorizes** the State Legislature to make any provision for reservation of seats or offices of chairperson in favor of backward classes. This is a voluntary provision, not a mandatory one under the central Act. **So, point III is not correct.**
● The 73rd Amendment Act mandates that not less than **one-third of the total number of seats** (including those reserved for SC/ST women) must be reserved for **women**. Additionally, not less than one-third of the offices of chairpersons at each level must be reserved for women. **So, point IV is correct.**
● The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act does not contain any provision for the reservation of seats for **Persons with Disabilities**. **So, point V is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the State Election Commission (SEC), consider the following statements:
I. 	 The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Governor, but the power to determine their tenure of office is vested in the State Legislature.
II. 	 The State Election Commissioner can be removed from office only in the manner and on the grounds prescribed for the removal of a judge of the High Court.
III. 	The superintendence, direction, and control of the preparation of electoral rolls for elections to the Panchayats are vested in the State Election Commission.
How many of the statements given above are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The State Election Commission is a constitutional body established under **Articles 243K and 243ZA** of the Constitution of India. It ensures the conduct of free, fair, and impartial elections to the local self-government bodies in the states.
● The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the **Governor**, and the Governor also has the authority to determine his/her **conditions of service and tenure** of office. While the Governor appoints the Commissioner and determines service conditions, the **State Legislature** has the power to make provisions with respect to all matters relating to **elections to the panchayats**. The statement incorrectly attributes the determination of tenure to the State Legislature. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● To ensure independence, the State Election Commissioner is provided with security of tenure. He/she cannot be removed from office except in the **manner and on the grounds** prescribed for the removal of a **judge of the state High Court**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The State Election Commission is vested with the power of **superintendence, direction, and control** of the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of all **elections to the panchayats**. However, elections to the **State Legislative Assembly** are conducted by the **Election Commission of India**, not the SEC. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following categories of goods:
I. 	 Engineering goods
II. 	 Electronic goods
III. 	Gems and jewellery
IV. 	 Refined petroleum products
How many of the above are major exports from
India to the United States of America (USA)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● According to official data from the Ministry of Commerce & Industry and the Press Information Bureau, the US accounted for **approximately 17.9%** (often rounded to 17% or 18%) of India's total merchandise exports in FY24 and FY25.
● India’s **major exports to the United States** include **electronic and electrical goods** such as smartphones and other equipment, **gems and jewellery,** pharmaceutical products including generic medicines, **engineering goods and machinery,** textiles and apparel, and **refined petroleum products**. Other significant exports include organic and inorganic chemicals, leather goods, and related merchandise. **So,** **points I, II, III and IV are correct.**
● India’s **imports from the US** rose from **US$ 42.19 billion in FY24** to **US$ 45.69 billion in FY25**, with **5,695 commodities** imported. The principal import categories comprised **mineral fuels and oils**, **pearls** **and precious/semi-precious stones and metals**, **machinery and mechanical appliances**, and **electrical** **machinery and equipment**. **So, all the four of the above are major exports from India to the United States of America (USA).** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Decentralised Procurement (DCP) Scheme in
India:
I. 	 Its implementation is mandatory for all States.
II. 	 Surplus foodgrains procured by States are
required to be handed over to the Food
Corporation of India (FCI).
III. 	States do not receive any reimbursement
from the Centre for procurement-related
expenditure.
Which of the statements given above are **not**
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Decentralised Procurement (DCP)** Scheme **was introduced in 1997–98** to enable State Governments to procure, store, and distribute foodgrains under the **Targeted Public Distribution System** **(TPDS)** and other welfare schemes.
○ The system allows State Governments to directly procure paddy/rice and wheat, and to store and distribute them under TPDS and other welfare schemes. It aims to ensure that MSP benefits reach the farmers, improve procurement efficiency, reduce transit losses and costs, and enable the distribution of grains suited to local preferences.
● The **Scheme is not mandatory;** adoption by States is voluntary and encouraged by the Centre. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● Under DCP**, State Governments procure foodgrains** on behalf of the Central Government. If the quantity procured exceeds the State’s allocation requirements, the surplus stock is handed over to the **Food** **Corporation of India (FCI),** which manages the central pool and inter-state movement. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● The expenditure incurred by States on procurement, collection, storage, and distribution is **reimbursed** **by the Central Government.** **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
The term extreme poverty, as defined by the World
Bank, primarily measures:

options.a:
Multidimensional deprivation in health and
education
options.b:
Income or consumption level below a global
threshold
options.d: Employment status in the informal sector

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The World Bank defines **extreme poverty** based on **income or consumption levels falling below a fixed** **global poverty line**.
● The current international poverty line for extreme poverty is **$3.00 per day in 2021 Purchasing Power** **Parity (PPP) terms**. This threshold reflects the typical national poverty lines of the world’s poorest countries and allows comparison across countries by adjusting for differences in price levels.
● Thus, extreme poverty is measured strictly in **monetary terms (income or consumption)** and does not directly assess multidimensional deprivation, nutritional deficiency, or employment status. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Financial Markets
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Tier II Bonds:
I. 	 They form part of a bank’s supplementary
capital under Basel III norms.
II. 	 They typically have a minimum maturity of
at least five years at the time of issue.
III. 	In the event of liquidation, their claims are
subordinated to those of depositors.
IV. 	 They carry a Put Option that investors can
exercise after five years.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tier II bonds** are subordinated debt instruments issued by banks to strengthen their supplementary capital under **Basel III norms.** They help improve the **Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR)**, which measures a bank’s financial strength and its ability to absorb losses. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Banks issue Subordinated Tier II bonds to augment their **Tier II capital** under regulatory capital adequacy norms. These instruments must have a **minimum original maturity of five years** at the time of issuance. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Being subordinated instruments, Tier II bondholders **rank below depositors, senior debt holders, and** **general creditors,** but above equity shareholders in the case of liquidation. Due to higher risk, they usually offer higher coupon rates than senior bonds. **So, statement III is correct.**
● Bank Tier II bonds **do not have a Put Option and only have a Call Option** (option for early repayment),
which the bank can exercise after a minimum of 5 years and after approval from the Reserve Bank of India (RBI).  **So, statement IV is not correct.**
○ **Call option**: This is a right held by the Bank. It allows the bank to "call back" or repay the debt early. The bank exercises this when interest rates fall so they can refinance at a cheaper rate. ○ **Put Option**: This is a right held by the bondholder. It allows the investor to force the bank to repay the principal before maturity. Investors exercise this when interest rates rise so they can take their money and reinvest it at a higher rate. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Banks are governed by strict and sound regulations under the **Banking Regulation Act, 1949.** Further, they come under the supervision and regulatory framework of the apex body i.e. **Reserve Bank of India**. Banks need to follow the **Basel-III norms**, which is a worldwide regulatory framework that manages financial stress, maintains market discipline and controls capital adequacy.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
The “52 Reforms in 52 Weeks” initiative is
associated with which one of the following sectors
in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The initiative **“52 Reforms in 52 Weeks”** is associated with reforms in **Indian Railways**. It was announced by the Ministry of Railways as a structured reform roadmap for 2026. The programme aims to introduce one major reform every week to systematically improve railway performance.
● Key focus areas include: ○ Enhancing on-board cleanliness and passenger amenities ○ Strengthening maintenance and repair mechanisms during journeys ○ Deploying AI-based monitoring systems ○ Expanding freight capacity through initiatives like Gati Shakti cargo terminals **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following factors:
I. 	 Strong demographic advantage and robust
domestic demand
II. 	 Sustained fiscal consolidation driven by
higher tax revenues
III. 	Focus on a single export destination
IV. 	 Low external debt-to-GDP ratio
How many of the above are favourable for a
sovereign credit rating upgrade of a country?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A sovereign credit rating upgrade signifies an improved assessment by an international rating agency of a country’s ability to meet its long-term debt obligations. In September 2025, Rating and Investment Information, Inc. (R&I), Japan, upgraded India’s long-term sovereign credit rating to **BBB+** from **BBB**, while retaining a **Stable Outlook**.
● The agency highlighted structural drivers such as India’s **demographic dividend**, **robust domestic** **demand**, and **sound government policies**, indicating that growth momentum was supported by fundamental economic strengths rather than cyclical factors alone. **So, point I is correct.**
● A key factor underpinning the upgrade was India’s **strengthened external stability**. The agency cited a **modest current account deficit**, stable surpluses in **services exports** and **remittances**, a **low external** **debt-to-GDP ratio**, and **sufficient foreign exchange reserves**. **So, point IV is correct.**
● The review emphasized India’s progress in **fiscal consolidation**. According to the agency, buoyant **tax** **revenues**, aided by strong domestic demand, and the **rationalisation of subsidies** enabled the government to reduce the **fiscal deficit**, even as it continued to increase **capital expenditures**. **So, point II is correct.**
● R&I assessed India’s **debt levels as manageable**, thereby improving debt sustainability dynamics.
● **High dependence on a single export destination** increases vulnerability to external shocks and demand fluctuations. **Following the recent increase in U.S. tariffs**, R&I noted that India’s **limited dependence** **on U.S. exports** and its **domestic demand-driven growth model** would likely mitigate adverse impacts. **So, point III is not correct.**
●  It further remarked that although **GST rationalisation** might lead to short-term revenue losses, the negative effects could be partially offset by **stimulated private consumption**.
● Policy measures aimed at attracting foreign manufacturers, infrastructure development, strengthening the legal and institutional framework, improving the business environment, and reducing reliance on energy imports. These reforms were viewed as supportive of long-term economic competitiveness and stability.
● This marked the **third upgrade for India within the same year**, following earlier upgrades by S&P in August 2025 and Morningstar DBRS in May 2025. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and Neighbourhood (Pakistan, China, Nepal, Sri Lanka, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
India is the largest milk-exporting country in the
world.
**Statement II:**
India’s milk production has increased by about
70% over the past decade.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India is the **world’s largest milk producer**, with 22 percent of global production, followed by the United States of America, Pakistan, China and Brazil. However, it **is not the largest exporter.** Due to its massive population, the majority of the milk produced is consumed domestically. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● India’s dairy sector has recorded significant expansion over the past decade. During the past eleven years, milk production increased from **146 million tonnes in 2014–15** to **239 million tonnes in 2023–24**, representing a growth of around **70 per cent**. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Key facts on milk production in India:** ○ India’s **milk processing capacity** currently stands at **660 lakh litres per day**, with a target of **100** **million litres per day by 2028–29**. This reflects efforts to expand value addition, reduce wastage, and enhance integration with organised markets. ○ Haryana continues to be a leading state in dairy performance; the state consistently ranks between **first and third** in **per capita milk availability**. ○ Haryana is the **third-largest milk producer** in the country, with an annual production of **122.2** **lakh tonnes** and per capita milk availability of **1,105 grams per day**. The state government highlighted ongoing support through **cooperative societies**, **low-interest loans**, **timely seed and** **fertiliser supply**, and **insurance and welfare schemes**, linking dairy growth with broader rural development objectives.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
**Economic Activity** **Sector**
I. - Cloud computing services Tertiary sector
II. - Electric vehicle manufacturing Secondary sector
III. - Offshore wind energy generation Primary sector
IV. - Commercial aquaculture Primary sector
How many of the above pairs are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Some economic activities share similar characteristics and based on this, they can be grouped together or classified into broader groups called **economic sectors.** The three main types of economic sectors are primary, secondary and tertiary economic sectors.
● **Cloud computing services** deliver digital infrastructure, storage, and software capabilities to users, making it a part of the Quaternary sector, which is treated as a "specialized sub-set" of the Tertiary sector. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
○ **Tertiary industries,** known as the service sector, provide services instead of goods. ○ **Cloud computing** is an online service that encompasses three service models, which are Softwareas-a-Service (SaaS), Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) and Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS).
●  **Electric vehicle manufacturing** is a factory-based industrial activity that converts inputs like steel, electronics, and batteries into vehicles, which is a part of the secondary sector. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
○ **Secondary industries** include manufacturing and processing activities that transform raw materials from the primary sector into finished goods.
● **Offshore wind energy generation** does not fall within the primary sector. The sector is exclusively concerned with obtaining raw materials directly from nature, including activities such as agriculture, mining, fishing, and forestry. Although wind itself is a natural resource, generating electricity through wind turbines is not merely extraction. Instead, it involves a technological process that converts natural energy into usable power, making it an **industrial activity** rather than a primary extraction activity. **So,** **pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Commercial aquaculture** involves the cultivation/harvesting of fish and other aquatic organisms. It falls under the **primary sector.**  **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
With reference to the military expeditions
of Rajendra Chola I, consider the following
statements:
I. 	 He conquered the southern regions of Ceylon
(Sri Lanka).
II. 	 He is known for the conquest and annexation
of the Maldives.
III. 	He launched a naval expedition against the
Srivijaya kingdom in Southeast Asia.
IV. 	 His northern campaign is often described
as the reverse of Samudragupta’s southern
expedition.
How many of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Chola Dynasty (9th-13th Century)** was a prominent Tamil dynasty that originated in Southern India and had a long-lasting influence on South Indian history. At its peak, the Cholas ruled the vast Chola Empire, a powerful maritime state that extended its influence across South and Southeast Asia.
● **Rajendra Chola I** capitalised on a revolt against the Sinhalese King Mahinda V and successfully **invaded** **southern Ceylon or Sri Lanka,** bringing the entire island under Chola administration for several decades, marking the peak of Chola territorial expansion. Earlier, his father, **Rajaraja I**, had **conquered northern** **Sri Lanka (Illamandalam)**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **naval campaign** resulting in the **conquest of the Maldive Islands** was actually achieved by his **father, Rajaraja I.** While Rajendra I **maintained** these territories and used them as strategic transit **points** **for trade**, the credit for the initial annexation of the Maldives and the **Malabar Coast** belongs to the elder Chola. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● In **1025 CE,** Rajendra Chola launched a **naval** **campaign** against the **Srivijaya kingdom** in Southeast Asia **(modern Indonesia/Malaysia).** The expedition targeted ports **controlling maritime trade routes,** restoring Indian merchant access to South China Sea networks. **So, statement III is correct.**
● Rajendra’s army marched along the **east coast to the Ganges River** in **1021–22 CE.** Historians often describe this as the **"reverse" of Samudragupta’s 4th-century campaign;** whereas the Gupta king marched from the **north to conquer the south (Kanchipuram),** Rajendra marched from the south to claim **victory in the north.** **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Prime Minister Narendra Modi visited Gangaikonda Cholapuram in Tamil Nadu during the valedictory ceremony of the Aadi Thiruvathirai festival to commemorate the birth anniversary of Emperor Rajendra Chola I.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with
reference to the Mamalla style of temple
architecture:
I. 	 It introduced pillars adorned with simha vyala
images in temples.
II. 	 It shows the transition from rock-cut
architecture to monolithic architecture.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In south India, rock-cut architecture laid the foundation for the Dravidian style of architecture. The Pallavas are credited with the introduction of this style. The evolution of the Pallava style of architecture corresponded with the reigning rulers, viz. Mahendravarman I, Narsimhavarman I, Rajsimhavarman and Aparajita.
● Pillars of the temples are one of the very important features of the Mamalla style. Narsimhavarman I or Mamalla is well known for his temple-building activities. **In the Mahendra style, they are thick, heavy** **and cubical in shape with corbel capitals.** As a departure, this style displays their better proportion. They are **tall, slender, more ornamented and appear elegant.** On their base, we find **leogryph (lion-** **headed creature) or vyala motif.** These **simha vyalas** are shown in a seated position providing support to the pillars. Hence, this style introduced a very vital feature of the Pallava architecture i.e. the **pillars** **adorned with simha vyala images. So, statement I is correct.**
● A crucial development in the field of Pallava architecture occurred during this style. It witnessed a **steady** **transition from the rock-cut style to the construction of monolithic structures.** He is well-known for the construction of the seven pagodas or the monolithic rathas at Mahabalipuram/Mamallapuram (Kanchipuram district, Tamil Nadu). These rathas are built out of monolithic rock. These are 10 in number out of which the five rathas – commonly known as pancha pandava rathas – are of more significance. These pancha rathas are: the **Draupadi ratha, Dharmaraja ratha, Bhima ratha, Arjuna ratha, and** **Nakul-Sahdeva ratha. So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Delhi Sultanate, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It arose in North India in the aftermath of the military campaigns of Muhammad Ghori.
II. 	 It institutionalised the Iqta system as an administrative and revenue arrangement.
III. 	It introduced a uniform paper currency across its territories.
IV. 	 It was responsible for introducing the Pietra Dura style of architecture in India.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Delhi Sultanate (1206–1526 CE)**, founded after the **Ghurid conquests**, comprised five dynasties: **Mamluk, Khalji, Tughluq, Sayyid and Lodi.** It introduced administrative innovations, **military** **centralisation** and fiscal experiments that shaped **medieval Indian governance.**
● **Muhammad Ghori** defeated Prithviraj Chauhan in the **Second Battle of Tarain (1192)**, opening the Indo-Gangetic plains to **Turkish expansion.** After his death in 1206, his general **Qutb-ud-din Aibak** declared independent rule, **founding the Delhi Sultanate. So, statement I is correct.**
● **Iltutmish** (1211–1236) institutionalised the **Iqta system**, granting revenue assignments to nobles **in lieu** **of salary.** Iqtadars collected land revenue but did not own the territory, strengthening **central authority** and ensuring **military support. So, statement II is correct.**
● The Delhi Sultanate **did not introduce a uniform paper currency.** It was known for its metallic coinage reforms, specifically the silver Tanka and copper Jital introduced by Iltutmish. While **Muhammad bin** **Tughlaq** famously attempted to introduce a **"token currency**" made of brass and copper, it was not paper and was a noted failure. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **Pietra Dura**, the technique of inlaying **semi-precious stones** into marble, was introduced in India during the **Mughal period**, especially under **Shah Jahan** in the **17th century,** prominently seen in the **Taj** **Mahal**, **not during the Delhi Sultanate. So, statement IV is not correct.** **So, only two of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
With reference to the song “Amar Shonar Bangla”, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It was written by Rabindranath Tagore.
II. 	 It was composed in the backdrop of the NonCooperation Movement.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **“Amar Shonar Bangla”** is a patriotic **Bengali song** written and composed by **Rabindranath Tagore** in **1905**. It was intended to inspire **unity among Bengalis** during the anti-partition agitation and became an iconic **cultural symbol of resistance.** It later became the **national anthem of Bangladesh. So, statement** **I is correct.**
● The song was **not** composed during the **Non-Cooperation Movement (1920–22).** It originated in **1905** as a response to the **Partition of Bengal** and was closely linked to the **Swadeshi boycott** campaign. **So,** **statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A controversy recently erupted in Assam after a political leader sang Bangladesh's national anthem, 'Amar Sonar Bangla,' at a political event.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Advent of Europeans, British Expansion, Anglo Wars
questions.questionText:
Consider the following events with regard to British territorial expansion around India:
I. 	 Annexation of Sindh
II. 	 Annexation of Burma
III. 	Annexation of Sri Lanka (Ceylon)
IV. 	 Acquisition of the Duars from Bhutan What is the correct chronological order of the above events, starting from the earliest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The British annexed **Sri Lanka (then Ceylon)** in **1815** following the **Kandyan Wars**, which ended over 2,300 years of **Sinhalese monarchical rule** and brought the **Kandyan Kingdom** under British control. While coastal areas were seized earlier in 1796, the full island annexation occurred in 1815, with **British** **rule lasting until 1948.**
● **Sindh** was formally **annexed** by the British East India Company in **1843**. Led by **Sir Charles Napier**, the British defeated the ruling **Talpur Amirs** at the **Battle of Miani (February 1843)** and the **Battle of** **Hyderabad.** The territory was subsequently integrated into the **Bombay Presidency.**
● The British acquired the **Duars from Bhutan** in **1865** following the **Bhutan War (1864–1865).** The formal annexation was finalized through the **Treaty of Sinchula,** signed on **November 11, 1865,** which forced Bhutan to cede the **eighteen Bengal and Assam Duars** to British India in exchange for an **annual** **subsidy.**
● The annexation of **Burma** by the British took place in **1886**. The **Third Anglo-Burmese War**, also known as the **Third Burma War**, was a conflict that took place from **7 to 29 November 1885.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: 1857 Revolt - Resistance & Revolt
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Movement Associated Region
l. Wahabi Movement Patna
II. - Indigo Revolt Bengal
III. - Koya Tribal Revolt Chotanagpur
IV. - Santhal Rebellion Rajmahal Hills
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Wahabi Movement** was a **19th-century Islamic revivalist** and socio-religious reform movement, centered in **Patna (Bihar)** from the **1820s to the 1870s,** aimed at purifying Islam and overthrowing **British/Sikh rule.** Founded by **Sayyid Ahmad Barelvi (1786–1831),** it sought to restore Islamic power by enforcing **strict monotheism** and rejecting **un-Islamic practices (bid'ah). So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● The **Indigo Revolt (1859-60),** or **Nil Bidroho**, was a massive peasant uprising in **Bengal** against forced **indigo cultivation** by European planters. Starting in **Nadia** in March 1859, farmers refused to sow indigo, using **non-violent resistance** and armed resistance to destroy factories. The revolt resulted in the **restriction of forced cultivation.** **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Koya Tribal Revolt** refers to a series of uprisings by the **Koya tribe** in the late 19th century against **British colonial policies,** the oppressive **Zamindari** system, and exploitation by **outsiders (known as** **dikus).** The most significant and organized phase occurred in 1879–1880, primarily in the **Eastern** **Godavari tract (modern-day Andhra Pradesh and parts of Odisha/Malkangiri). So, pair III is not** **correctly matched.**
● The **Santhal Rebellion (1855–1856)**, known as **Santhal Hul,** was a massive, early anti-colonial uprising by **tribal peasants** against British authorities, zamindars, and moneylenders. Originating on June 30, 1855, in the **Damin-i-Koh region (Rajmahal Hills, Jharkhand)**, it was led by four brothers, **Sidhu,** **Kanhu, Chand, and Bhairav Murmu.** **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
With reference to Vajrayana Buddhism, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It originated in Southeast Asia during the first century CE.
II. 	 It considers tantric methods as a rapid path to enlightenment.
III. 	It rejects the ideal of Bodhisattva which is central to Mahayana Buddhism.
IV. 	 It places great emphasis on the guru–shishya parampara for spiritual progress.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Vajrayana Buddhism**, also known as **Tantric or Esoteric Buddhism**, developed in India around the **6th–7th century CE** as an advanced form of **Mahayana Buddhism**. It later spread to **Tibet, Mongolia,** **Nepal and Japan.**
● Vajrayana **originated in India**, not Southeast Asia. It flourished between the **6th and 11th centuries CE** and later spread to **Tibet** through teachers like **Padmasambhava** and to **Japan** through the **Shingon** **tradition. So, statement I is not correct.**
● Vajrayana teaches that **tantric practices** such as **deity yoga**, **mantra recitation**, **visualization** and ritual initiation can enable **enlightenment within a single lifetime,** unlike gradual **multi-lifetime paths** emphasised in earlier traditions. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Vajrayana is a **branch of Mahayana Buddhism** and fully **accepts the Bodhisattva** ideal. It emphasises attaining **Buddhahood** for the benefit of all beings, integrating **compassion (karuna)** and **wisdom** **(prajna). So, statement III is not correct.**
● Vajrayana stresses the necessity of **initiation (abhisheka)** from a **qualified guru.** Tantric teachings are transmitted through **lineage**, and **proper guidance** is considered essential due to the **complexity** and **esoteric** nature of practices. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Which of the following dynasties existed before the 10th century CE in India?
I. 	 Ahom
II. 	 Haryanka
III. 	Sunga
IV. 	 Kakatiya
V. 	 Kushan
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.b: I, II, III and V only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Ahom dynasty** was founded in **1228 CE** by **Sukaphaa** in **present-day Assam.** It ruled for nearly six centuries, successfully resisting **Mughal expansion** in Northeast India. Since it emerged in the **13th** **century,** it did not exist before the 10th century CE. **So, point I is not correct.**
● The **Haryanka dynasty (6th century BCE)** was one of the earliest ruling dynasties of Magadha. Founded by **Bimbisara**, it expanded **Magadha's** power through conquests and alliances. His son **Ajatashatru** further strengthened the kingdom. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **Sunga dynasty (185–73 BCE)** was founded by **Pushyamitra Sunga** after overthrowing the last Mauryan ruler, **Brihadratha**. It ruled northern and central India, revived Brahmanical traditions, and faced **Indo-Greek incursions** during its reign. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **Kakatiya dynasty** emerged in the **12th century CE** in the regions of present-day Telangana and Andhra Pradesh. Initially feudatories of the **Western Chalukyas**, they later established independent rule under **Prola II** and **Ganapati Deva**. Their capital was Warangal. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● The **Kushan dynasty (1st–3rd century CE)**, founded by **Kujula Kadphises**, established a vast empire across **northwestern India** and Central Asia. Under Kanishka I, it promoted Mahayana Buddhism and facilitated **Indo-Roman and Silk Route** trade networks. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Council of Ministers during the Mauryan
rule:
I. 	 It was an advisory body and the King had the
power to overrule their recommendations.
II. 	 They could discuss the King's policy during
his absence and decide upon any important
matter which the King had left to them.
III. 	Arthasastra mentions the criteria for the
appointment of the ministers.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Arthasastra as well as the Asokan inscriptions refer to a **Council of Ministers (Mantriparishad).** The Arthasastra mentions that the work of the state cannot be carried out without assistance.
● Rock Edict VI mentions that the **ministers can discuss the King's policy** **during his absence;** suggest amendments; and **decide upon any important matter which the King has left to them.** Yet the Council had to report its opinion to the King immediately. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The power of the Council might have varied from time to time, yet its primary role remained that of an **advisory body**. This was because the **final authority was vested with the King**. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Arthasastra mentions the** **criteria or the qualifications for the appointment of ministers**. For example, the person should be such as not to be lured by wealth, not to succumb to pressure, etc. i.e., he should be a **Sarvopdashudha (purest of all).** **So, statement III is correct.**
● The **Girnar Rock Edicts of Asoka** mention the **functions of the Council**. Similarly, **Rock Edict III** implies that the Parishad was expected to see that new administrative measures were carried out by different categories of officials.
● There are references about **eighteen departments (Tirthas)** of the Central Government in the Arthasastra. For example the Karmantika looked after the industries, the Antarvamsika looked after the King's security and the Sannidhata looked after the treasury, etc. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Painted Grey Ware culture is primarily associated
with which one of the following periods?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Painted Grey Ware (PGW)** culture is dated roughly to **1100–400 BCE** (with some sites extending up to 1400 BCE) and is mainly associated with the **Later Vedic phase** in North India. It is identified by **fine** **grey pottery** made on a fast wheel and painted with black geometric designs such as **concentric circles,** **dots, spirals, checks, and swastika-like symbols.**
● PGW sites are widely distributed across the **Upper Gangetic plains**, the Indo-Gangetic divide and the **Sutlej basin,** covering present-day **Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh and Rajasthan**, and parts of **eastern Pakistan**.
● **Important sites include** **Hastinapur**, **Ahichchhatra**, **Alamgirpur**, Mathura, Kampil, Kaushambi, Shravasti, Bhagwanpura and **Jakhera**.
● Archaeological evidence shows **iron tools like ploughs, sickles and hoes**, along with terracotta figurines, beads, copper objects and ivory items, indicating **settled agro-pastoral communities.** PGW was later succeeded by Northern Black Polished Ware (NBPW) linked with **early urbanisation.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Lunar Module Launch
Vehicle, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It uses both liquid and cryogenic propellants.
II. 	 It cannot carry payloads beyond the Low
Earth Orbit.
III. 	It aims to replace the PSLV for all satellite
launches.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Lunar Module Launch Vehicle (LMLV)** is ISRO’s upcoming **super-heavy-lift rocket,** planned by **2035**, intended to support **crewed lunar missions** and **deep space exploration** with significantly higher payload capacity.
● The LMLV is planned as a **three-stage rocket**, with the **first two** stages using **liquid propellants** and the **upper stage** using advanced **cryogenic propulsion** for high-efficiency deep space missions. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The LMLV would be capable of carrying about **27 tonnes to the Moon** and **80 tonnes to low Earth** **orbits**, which are between **200 and 2,000 km** from the planet’s surface. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The LMLV is designed for **lunar and heavy interplanetary missions**, not routine satellite launches. **PSLV will continue serving** as India’s reliable workhorse for medium and small satellite deployments. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The ISRO has announced the development of its heaviest rocket ever, the Lunar Module Launch Vehicle (LMLV), expected to be ready by 2035.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following best describes
Hawking radiation?

options.a:
Radiation released from neutron stars during
supernova explosions
options.b:
Radiation emitted due to nuclear fusion
occurring inside a black hole
options.c:
Electromagnetic waves produced when black
holes collide
options.d:
Radiation emitted by black holes due to
quantum effects near the event horizon

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Hawking radiation** is a theoretical prediction made in **1974** by physicist **Stephen Hawking,** combining principles of **quantum mechanics** and **general relativity.**
● According to this concept, **black holes** are not completely “black” but **emit weak thermal radiation** due to **quantum effects** occurring near the **event horizon**, the boundary beyond which nothing can escape. In **quantum field theory, particle–antiparticle pairs** constantly form and annihilate in the vacuum.
● If such a pair forms near the event horizon, **one particle may fall into the black hole** while the other escapes, appearing as radiation to an **outside observer.** This process causes the black hole to **gradually** **lose mass and energy,** leading to a slow evaporation over immense timescales.
● **Smaller black holes** emit radiation more intensely because their temperature is inversely proportional to their mass. Although Hawking radiation **has not yet been directly observed,** it remains a crucial **theoretical bridge** between gravity and quantum physics. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, scientists proposed that morsel black holes emit Hawking radiation at an increasing rate as they lose mass, leading to their explosive demise.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
With reference to malaria, consider the following
statements:
I. 	 Plasmodium parasites first infect the liver
before invading red blood cells.
II. 	 AdFalciVax is India’s first indigenously
developed malaria vaccine.
III. 	India has set a target of achieving zero
indigenous malaria cases by 2035.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Malaria** is a **mosquito-borne parasitic disease** caused by **Plasmodium species** and transmitted through the **bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.** It remains a major public health concern in tropical regions, including parts of India.
● Malaria **does not spread** through casual **person-to-person contact.** Transmission primarily occurs through the **bite of an infected mosquito**, though rare cases may occur via blood transfusion or organ transplant.
● After entering the **bloodstream**, Plasmodium sporozoites **infect liver cells (pre-erythrocytic stage)**, multiply, and then release merozoites that **invade red blood cells,** leading to clinical symptoms such as fever and anemia. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **AdFalciVax** is **India’s first indigenously developed** malaria **vaccine** targeting Plasmodium falciparum, designed to interrupt **both liver-stage infection** and **transmission**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● India has set an **ambitious target** of achieving **zero indigenous cases by 2030.** This goal is guided by the **National Framework for Malaria Elimination (2016–2030)** and the National Strategic Plan (2023– 2027).  **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) has granted non-exclusive rights for its multi-stage malaria vaccine, AdFalciVax, to five pharmaceutical companies.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to the HAMMER smart weapons
system sometimes seen in the news, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 It is a precision-guided glide bomb developed
by France.
II. 	 It can be deployed from both fighter aircraft
and naval platforms.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **HAMMER (Highly Agile and Manoeuvrable Munition Extended Range)**, also known as **AASM**, is a **French-developed precision-guided air-to-ground weapon.** India and France are collaborating for its **joint production** under defence cooperation initiatives. HAMMER is a **modular** precision-guided **glide bomb** kit that converts standard **250 kg, 500 kg, and 1,000 kg** unguided bombs into smart munitions with ranges up to 70 km. **So, statement I is correct.**
● HAMMER is an **air-to-surface weapon** launched solely from aircraft, **not directly from naval** **platforms.** While intended for naval aviation use via the Rafale-M, it is not a ship- or submarine-launched missile. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Bharat Electronics Limited and Safran Electronics and Defence (SED) signed a joint venture cooperation agreement for the production of the HAMMER Weapon System in India.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Map-Based Relevance (Trade Routes, Ports, Countries, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the “3 by 35” Initiative, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 It was launched by the United Nations
Environment Programme (UNEP).
II. 	 It aims to restore 3% of the Earth’s degraded
peatland ecosystems by 2035.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **“3 by 35” Initiative** was launched by the **World Health Organization (WHO)**, not the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP). It focuses on **increasing taxes on tobacco, alcohol, and** **sugary drinks** to improve public health and mobilize domestic revenues. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The 3 by 35 Initiative urges countries to **raise real prices** on tobacco, alcohol, and sugary drinks by **at** **least 50 per cent by 2035.** The initiative has an ambitious goal of raising **US $1 trillion over the next 10** **years.**  It aims to **revitalize health taxes** as a powerful tool to **reduce harmful consumption,** save lives, and generate vital public revenue. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the World Health Organization (WHO) has launched a”3 by 35” initiative.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the DRAVYA Portal, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 It has been developed by the Ministry of
Electronics and Information Technology
(MeitY) as part of the Digital India initiative.
II. 	 It provides verified information on
AYUSH substances, including botanical,
pharmacological, and chemical data.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **DRAVYA Portal** is a **digital platform** developed to create a comprehensive **database of AYUSH** **substances**, integrating classical **Ayurvedic knowledge** with verified scientific data for **research,** **academic, and public use.**
● The DRAVYA Portal was developed by the **Central Council for Research in Ayurvedic Sciences** **(CCRAS)** under the **Ministry of Ayush, (not by MeitY).** It was launched on 23 September 2025 on Ayurveda Day. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The portal provides **authenticated information** on **AYUSH substances**, covering Ayurveda descriptions, **botany, chemistry, pharmacy, and pharmacology**. Data on **378 medicinal plants** has been compiled from authoritative classical texts and verified digital research sources. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Ministry of Ayush has recently launched the DRAVYA portal (Digitised Retrieval Application for Versatile Yardstick of Ayush) in Goa.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Vikram 3201, India’s first fully indigenous 32-bit
microprocessor, is primarily designed to:

options.a: Use in common household electronic devices
options.b:
Support aerospace, satellite, and launch
vehicle applications
options.c:
Replace commercial processors used in Indian
defence systems
options.d: Function solely as a telecommunication chip

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Vikram-32 (VIKRAM3201)** is India’s first fully indigenous, space-grade 32-bit microprocessor, specifically developed for use in space applications. It was designed by the **Vikram Sarabhai Space** **Centre (VSSC)** and fabricated at ISRO’s Semiconductor Laboratory (SCL), Chandigarh, using a 180 nm CMOS process.
● The chip is specifically built for **launch vehicle avionics, handling navigation, control,** and **mission** **management** functions that require high reliability and real-time processing. It succeeds the earlier **16-** **bit Vikram 1601 processor** used since 2009.
● Vikram-32 supports **64-bit floating-point operations** and **Ada programming language** compatibility, essential for aerospace-grade computation. It is engineered to withstand **extreme space conditions**, including temperatures ranging from **–55°C to +125°C, high vibration**, and radiation exposure.
● The processor was unveiled at **Semicon India 2025** and represents a major step under the India Semiconductor Mission toward **technological self-reliance in high-reliability aerospace electronics.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India unveiled its first fully indigenous 32-bit microprocessor, the Vikram 3201, at the Semicon India 2025 conference recently.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Volcano Type Country
I. - Mount Vesuvius Cinder cone Spain
II. - Mount Merapi Stratovolcano Indonesia
III. - Kilauea Shield Malaysia
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mount Vesuvius** is located in **Italy**. It is a **stratovolcano**, characterised by alternating layers of lava and ash and known for explosive eruptions. **So, row I is not correctly matched**
● **Mount Merapi** is situated in **Central Java, Indonesia**, and is among the most active volcanoes globally. It is a classic **stratovolcano**, formed by viscous lava, pyroclastic material, and repeated explosive eruptions. Frequent eruptions, fertile volcanic soils, and dense human settlements on its slopes make it both hazardous and economically significant. **So, row II is correctly matched**
● **Kilauea** is located in **Hawaii, USA**. It is a **shield volcano**, built by fluid basaltic lava flows that create broad, gently sloping landforms. It is one of the world’s most active volcanoes, with long-duration eruptions affecting settlements on Hawaii’s Big Island. **So, row III is not correctly matched** **So, in only one of the above rows the given information is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Authorities recently recorded massive night lava avalanches cascading down the Merapi western slopes.

questions.subject: Geography

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Tundra region always experiences low
temperatures while deserts may be hot or
cold.
II. 	 Deserts have more diversity of vegetation as
compared to the tundra region.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **polar climate is characterized by a very low mean annual temperature**. Normally not more than four months have temperatures above freezing point in Tundra regions. While the **Deserts** on the basis of temperature are **classified into two types**: **Hot Desert** (E.g: Sahara Desert) and **Cold Desert** (E.g: Gobi Desert). **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **vegetation of hot deserts** is xerophytic or drought-resistant. It **includes the cacti, thorny bushes,** **scattered dwarf acacia** and long-rooted wiry grasses. Whereas **in Tundra, only mosses, lichens** and **ephemeral flowering plants (in summer)** can be observed. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography

questions.questionText:
With reference to Monazite, consider the following
statements:
I. 	 It is a phosphate mineral that serves as a
primary ore for rare earth elements.
II. 	 It is primarily found in coastal beach sands
and teri formations.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not**
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Monazite** commonly occurs in **coastal beach sands**, especially along the eastern and western coasts of India. Wave and current action concentrate heavy minerals like monazite into placer deposits. Monazite is also present in **teri (red sand) formations**, particularly in parts of southern India such as **Tamil Nadu**. These red sands are ancient coastal or aeolian deposits where heavy minerals, including monazite, became concentrated over geological time. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Monazite is chemically a **phosphate mineral** and is one of the principal ores of **rare earth elements** **(REEs),** Uranium and Thorium. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Besides coastal placers, monazite can occur in **inland alluvial deposits** formed by river transport and sediment deposition. Over time, heavy minerals get concentrated in riverine sediments, making inland occurrences possible. **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**What are the Beach Sand Minerals (BSM)?**
● In India, Beach Sand Minerals (BSM) is the **principal ore of Rare Earths (RE)**. In BSM ore, the prescribed **substance monazite occurs**, which is a phosphate mineral containing Uranium and Thorium.
● These deposits occur across Odisha, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, West Bengal, Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Jharkhand, primarily in coastal beach sands, teri/red sands, and inland alluvium.
**Relevance:** Union Finance Minister proposed establishment of dedicated rare earth corridors in India’s coastal states Odisha, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and Neighbourhood (Pakistan, China, Nepal, Sri Lanka, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following mountain passes:
I. 	 Karakoram
II. 	 Zoji La
III. 	Baralacha La
IV. 	 Bomdi La
V. 	 Diphu
The ancient Silk Route passed through how many
of the above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Silk Route (Silk Road)** was an ancient network of trade routes linking India with Central Asia, Tibet, and China. Through these routes, traders exchanged silk, spices, horses, precious stones, and also ideas such as religion and culture. In the Indian context, the Silk Route passed through certain high-altitude Himalayan and Trans-Himalayan passes that connected the subcontinent with Inner Asia.
● The **Karakoram Pass** lies in the Karakoram Range and historically connected **Ladakh with China**. It was one of the most important trans-Himalayan gateways used by traders moving between India and Central Asia. Because of this strategic linkage, it formed a significant segment of the Silk Route. **So, point** **I is correct.**
● **Zoji La** connects the Kashmir Valley with Ladakh. It served as an important gateway enabling traders from the Indian plains and Kashmir to access Ladakh, from where caravans moved onward to Tibet and Central Asia. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Baralacha La**, located in Himachal Pradesh, linked trade routes between **Lahaul, Spiti, and Ladakh**. It was used by traders transporting goods toward Tibet and Central Asia. Historical caravan movement across this pass places it within the Indian stretch of the Silk Route system. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Bomdi La** is a pass in Arunachal Pradesh connecting Arunachal Pradesh with Lhasa. While it has regional strategic and cultural importance, it was **not part of the classical trans-Himalayan Silk Route network.** **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Diphu Pass** lies near the India–China–Myanmar trijunction in Arunachal Pradesh. It is geographically significant but **not associated with the historic Silk Route trade corridors. So, point V is not  correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:**  Before highways, railways, and shipping lanes, India was covered by ancient trade routes that carried spices, silk, salt, precious stones, ideas, and beliefs across continents.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and Neighbourhood (Pakistan, China, Nepal, Sri Lanka, etc.)
questions.questionText: Aksai Chin is situated to the:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Aksai Chin refers to the disputed region in the northeastern part of India’s Union Territory of** **Ladakh.** It is almost the size of Bhutan. It is a cold desert of salt flatlands called the Soda Plains. It is watered by the Karakash, the Black jade river. It is currently occupied by **China which claims it to be a** **part of Xinjian province. India lays its claims over this region.**
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Pangong Lake also known as Pangong Tso is an endorheic lake situated in the Himalayas** **extending from India to China.** It is situated at a height of 4350 m. It is a **saline lake**. It freezes during the winter season. It is **situated on the Line of Actual Control (LAC)** between India and China. **Aksai** **Chin is located to the North and East of Pangong Tso.**
● The **Kunlun Mountains** are one of Asia’s longest mountain ranges. They start from the Pamir Mountains in the Republic of Tajikistan and extend across the western part of China spanning the Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region, the Tibet Autonomous Region, and the Chinese province of Qinghai. The Kunlun Mountains stand parallel to the Tian Shan Mountains in the north and the Himalayan mountains in the south. **Aksai Chin is located to the south of the Kunlun Mountains.**
● **Galwan valley refers to the land which is strategically located between Ladakh in the west and** **Aksai Chin in the east.** It is located between steep mountains along the Galwan River. The river has its source in Aksai Chin, on China’s side of the LAC, and it flows from the east to Ladakh. Shyok river and the Darbuka-Shyok-Daulet Beg Oldie (DSDBO) road are located on the west side of the valley. **Aksai Chin is located on the east side of the valley.**
● **Daulat Beg Oldi (DBO) is located in Ladakh west of the LAC at Aksai Chin.**  It is the **highest** **airfield in the world**. A **military outpost** was created at DBO after the Chinese occupation at Aksai Chin during the Sino-Indian war of 1962.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Orobanche weed, seen in the news recently, is
harmful to which one of the following crops?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● **Orobanche aegyptiaca:** This is a **parasitic weed** that attaches to the **roots of mustard plants and extracts** **nutrients**, carbon and water from them.
● By depriving the host crop of these, it **causes wilting,** **yellowing and stunted growth** of the plants and, thereby, lower mustard seed yields.
● A single Orobanche shoot bears **numerous purple** **flowers**, each producing thousands of extremely small seeds. These seeds can **remain viable in the** **soil for many years and spread easily through** **wind and water**, allowing the parasite to reach new fields. Over time, a persistent soil seed bank develops, creating conditions for severe and recurring infestation.
● **In mustard cultivation, the first irrigation is typically applied a few weeks after sowing, and the** **resulting soil moisture also favours the germination of Orobanche seeds**. The parasite then establishes itself underground and attaches to the roots of the mustard plant, drawing nutrients and weakening the host crop. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Orobanche has recently been reported as a major threat to mustard cultivation, raising concerns over crop losses and farmer livelihoods in key mustard-producing states.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Agriculture (Schemes, APMC, MSP, Exports, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** In winters, the north and northwestern India generally experience light to moderate rainfall.
**Statement II:** In winters, north and northwestern India is under the influence of Western Disturbances.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **a** **Explanation**:
● During the **winter season**, many parts of **north and north-west India**, such as **Punjab, Haryana,** **western Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Himachal Pradesh and Jammu & Kashmir** receive **light to** **moderate rainfall**. This winter rainfall is very important for agriculture, especially for **rabi crops like** **wheat**, which depend on moisture during this period. **So,** s**tatement I is correct**.
● This rainfall is mainly caused by **Western Disturbances**. These are weather systems that originate over the **Mediterranean region** and move eastwards towards India. When Western Disturbances pass through north and northwest India, they **interact with moisture-laden winds** coming from the **Arabian Sea** and sometimes the **Bay of Bengal**. This interaction leads to cloud formation and precipitation during winter. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
