questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Major Project River
I. - Mekedatu Krishna
II. - Almatti Cauvery
III. - Polavaram Godavari
IV. - Kaleshwaram Pranahita
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Mekedatu Project** is proposed across the **Cauvery River** near the Karnataka–Tamil Nadu border. It is planned mainly to meet **drinking water needs** of Bengaluru and nearby regions, along with some power generation. **So,** **pair I is not correctly matched**.
● The **Almatti Dam** is an irrigation and storage project built on the **Krishna River** in Karnataka. It supports irrigation, power generation, and regulation of water flows within the Krishna basin. **So,** **pair II is not** **correctly matched**.
● The **Polavaram Project** is a large **multipurpose irrigation project** constructed on the **Godavari River** in Andhra Pradesh. Its objectives include irrigation expansion, drinking water supply, flood control, and hydropower generation. **So**, **pair III is correctly matched**.
● The **Kaleshwaram Lift Irrigation Project** sources water mainly from the **Pranahita River**, which is a major tributary of the Godavari. The project uses lift irrigation to supply water to drought-prone areas of Telangana and is one of the world’s largest lift irrigation schemes. **So**, **pair IV is correctly matched**. **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union government has allocated revised funding in the latest budget for completing the Polavaram Irrigation Project.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following phenomena:
I. 	 A ship sailing away from the shore disappears hull-first before the mast
II. 	 The shadow casted by the Earth on the Moon during a lunar eclipse
III. 	Increase in the visibility of distant objects with an increase in height
IV. 	 Difference in the time zones and variation in sunrise and sunset timings
How many of the above provide evidence for the spherical shape of the Earth?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **d**
**Explanation:**
● The Earth is nearly a sphere. It is because of the fact that the equatorial radius and the polar radius of the earth is not the same. The rotation of the earth over its axis produces bulging at the equator. Hence, the actual shape resembles that of an **oblate spheroid.**
● One of the simplest observations supporting the spherical shape of the Earth is that **ships sailing away** **from the shore disappear hull-first and not uniformly**. This occurs because the ship moves beyond the curved surface of the Earth. **So, point I is correct.**
● During a **lunar eclipse**, the Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon and casts a **circular shadow** on the Moon. A consistently round shadow can only be produced by a spherical object. **So, point II is correct.**
● When a person climbs to a higher elevation, such as a tree, hill, or tall building, the **line of sight to the** **horizon extends**. This happens because more of the Earth’s curved surface becomes visible with height. **So, point III is correct.**
● The presence of **time zones and differences in sunrise and sunset timings across longitudes** arises because the Earth is spherical and rotates on its axis. If the Earth were flat, such systematic time differences would not exist. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, all four of the above phenomena provide evidence for the spherical shape of the Earth.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the difference between African and Asiatic cheetahs:
I. 	 African cheetahs are spread across Africa, whereas Asiatic cheetahs are confined to a limited region between Iran and Pakistan.
II. 	 African cheetahs are listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List, while Asiatic cheetahs are listed as Critically Endangered.
III. 	Unlike Asiatic cheetahs, African cheetahs are smaller and have thicker coats.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**: The **cheetah** is a prominent cat family member that belongs to the subfamily Felinae and is found across Africa and some parts of Iran. The cheetah prefers grassland habitats where it has enough space to go after its prey without any obstacles. The cheetah population forms keystone species in the grassland ecosystem. Thus it is a crucial species for the ecosystem.
● **African cheetahs are spread out across Africa** from Northwest Africa, East Africa, and Southern Africa. On the other hand, **Asiatic Cheetahs are now only found in Iran.** With a bigger territory, the African cheetahs have the highest populations compared to their Asiatic counterparts. Asiatic cheetahs once roamed the whole expanse of the central Asian continent from India to Afghanistan and Pakistan, but with their numbers reduced, it is hard for them to be sighted outside Iran. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **African Cheetahs**, with several subspecies occurring in different African countries, are listed as a **vulnerable (VU) species on the IUCN Red List of Threatened species.** On the other hand, **Asiatic** **cheetahs are** listed as **critically endangered** species on the IUCN Red List. The Asiatic cheetah is facing extinction. Loss of habitat is the leading cause behind the dwindling numbers of cheetahs in the wild. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **As compared to African cheetahs**, **Asiatic cheetahs are smaller but have thicker coats, powerful** **necks and slender legs.** Researchers believe that Asiatic cheetahs could be faster than the African species with longer legs, but no tests have confirmed this theory. **So, statement I is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Tiger Reserve Associated River State
I. - Veerangana Durgavati Tiger Reserve
Narmada Madhya Pradesh
II. - Madhav Tiger Reserve Manihar Rajasthan
III. - Dudhwa Tiger Reserve Suheli Uttar Pradesh In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Tiger Reserves in India are designated under **Project Tiger** to ensure the conservation of Bengal tigers along with their habitats.
● The **Veerangana Durgavati Tiger Reserve** is located in **Madhya Pradesh** and includes areas of Nauradehi and Durgavati Wildlife Sanctuaries. Geographically, the reserve spans parts of the **Narmada** **river basin** (and also touches the Yamuna basin through tributary systems). **So, row I is correctly** **matched**
● The **Madhav Tiger Reserve** (recently declared from Madhav National Park) is situated in **Madhya** **Pradesh**. The park is associated with rivers like the **Sindh and Manihar**, along with lakes such as Sakhya Sagar and Madhav Sagar. The **Banas River** flows in Rajasthan and has no connection with this reserve. **So, row II is not correctly matched**
● The **Dudhwa Tiger Reserve** lies in **Uttar Pradesh** along the Indo-Nepal border in the Terai region. The landscape is an alluvial plain interlaced with rivers and streams, including the **Suheli River** along with Mohana, Sharda, etc. **So, row III is correctly matched** **So, in only two of the above rows the given information is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**.
**Relevance:** The Union Government recently declared the Madhav National Park in Madhya Pradesh as the country’s 58th tiger reserve.

questions.questionText:
It is a method of extracting coal from narrow, horizontal seams, prevalent in Meghalaya. It has narrow pits dug into the ground, typically just large enough for one person to descend and extract coal.
Which type of mining is described above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **c** **Explanation**:
● **Rat-hole mining** is a **traditional and highly risky method of coal extraction** that has been widely practiced in **Meghalaya**, particularly in areas where coal seams are **thin and lie horizontally** close to the surface.
● In this method, **small vertical shafts** are first dug from the ground. From these pits, **very narrow** **horizontal tunnels** are cut along the coal seam. These tunnels are often **just wide enough for one person** **to crawl through**, which is why the method is called rat-hole mining. Coal is removed **manually**, usually **without proper safety gear, ventilation, or scientific planning**.
● This practice poses **serious dangers to miners**, including **tunnel collapses, flooding, toxic gas exposure,** **and suffocation**. It has also led to **severe environmental damage**, such as **contamination of** **groundwater, destruction of land, and pollution of nearby rivers**. Due to these risks, **the National** **Green Tribunal (NGT) banned rat-hole mining**, though concerns over **illegal mining and livelihood** **issues** continue to arise. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**What are the other types of mining?**
● **Open-cast mining** involves removing large quantities of overburden and is used for shallow coal deposits.
● **Longwall mining** is a modern, mechanised underground mining method.
● **Strip mining** is a surface mining technique used mainly for flat-lying mineral deposits.
**Relevance:** Recently, several miners have been killed in a deadly explosion inside an illegal rat-hole mine of Meghalaya’s East Jaintia Hills district.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText: Which one of the following best describes the term Frontline community?

options.a: Communities living near protected forests that assist in wildlife conservation
options.b: Communities that depend primarily on natural resources for their livelihood
options.c: Communities that experience climate change first and often suffer its most severe impacts
options.d: Communities located in regions with the highest biodiversity

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● Frontline communities are those communities that **experience climate change first and often feel the** **worst effects.** They are communities that have higher exposures, are more sensitive, and are less able to adapt to climate change impacts for a variety of reasons.
● Frontline communities include people who are **both highly exposed to climate risks** (because of the places they live and the projected changes expected to occur in those places) and **have fewer resources,** **capacity, safety nets, or political power** to respond to those risks (e.g. these people may lack insurance or savings, inflexible jobs, low levels of influence over elected officials, etc.). **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
Radiative forcing is the net change in the energy balance on the Earth due to various factors of climate change. In this context, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Negative radiative forcing results in a cooling effect on the Earth’s climate.
II. 	 Carbon dioxide accounts for the largest share of radiative forcing in the last few decades.
III. 	Stratospheric ozone contributes to positive radiative forcing.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A variety of physical and chemical changes can affect the global energy balance and force changes in the Earth’s climate. Some of these changes are natural, while others are influenced by humans. These changes are **measured by the amount of warming or cooling they produce, called “radiative forcing.”** Simply, Radiative Forcings is the **net change in the energy balance of the Earth system** due to various natural and anthropogenic factors of climate change. It is usually expressed in watts per square meter averaged over a particular period of time.
● **Changes that have a warming effect are called “positive” forcing, while changes that have a cooling** **effect are called “negative” forcing.** When positive and negative forces are out of balance, the result is a change in the Earth’s average surface temperature. **So, statement I is correct**.
● In 2019, the Annual Greenhouse Gas Index was 1.45, which represents a 45-per cent increase in radiative forcing (a net warming influence) since 1990. Of the greenhouse gases, **carbon dioxide accounts for by** **far the largest share** of radiative forcing since 1990, and its contribution continues to grow at a steady rate. Carbon dioxide alone would account for a 36-per cent increase in radiative forcing since 1990.  **So,** **statement II is correct**.
● **Ozone present in the stratosphere (above the troposphere) absorbs the incoming shortwave** **radiation.** Hence, it restricts the increase in temperature on the ground surface. As a result, this process **produces a negative radiative forcing** that is a cooling effect. But the **ozone formed in the troposphere** **increases the temperature near the ground surface** by absorbing the radiation. This results in positive radiative forcing. **So, statement III is not correct**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
With reference to ungulates found in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Sambar is the most abundant ungulate across the forests of India.
II. 	 Nilgai is India’s largest antelope and is highly adaptable to human-dominated landscapes.
III. 	Spotted deer (Chital) is mostly found in grasslands of Northeast India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ungulates are hoofed mammals** such as deer, antelopes, and wild bovids. India hosts a rich diversity of ungulates adapted to forests, grasslands, and even human-dominated landscapes.
● The **Spotted deer (Chital)** is the **most abundant ungulate** across the forests of India. Chital occur in large herds and thrive in diverse forest habitats, making them the most numerous forest ungulates in India. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Nilgai (Blue bull) is the largest antelope in India**. It is highly adaptable and commonly found near agricultural fields, scrublands, and village fringes. Its ability to survive in modified landscapes often leads to human–wildlife conflict, especially crop damage. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Spotted deer (Chital)** are **most abundant in the terai grasslands and deciduous forests** of North, Central, and South India. It is **rarely found in Northeast** India. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Spotted deer, sambar, and gaur populations are thriving in large parts of India, but declining in Odisha, Jharkhand, and Chhattisgarh.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
State Special Provision
I. - Nagaland Special responsibility to the Governor with respect to law and order
II. - Sikkim One seat allotted in the Lok Sabha
III. Goa Legislative Assembly is to consist of not less than 30 members
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 371-A** of the **Constitution of India** makes the following special provisions for **Nagaland:**
● The Acts of Parliament relating to the following matters would not apply to Nagaland unless the State Legislative Assembly so decides:
○ religious or social practices of the Nagas; ○ Naga customary law and procedure; ○ administration of civil and criminal justice involving decisions according to Naga customary law; and ○ ownership and transfer of land and its resources.
● The **Governor** has to ensure that the **money provided by the Central Government** for **any specific** **purpose is included in the demand for a grant relating to that purpose** and not in any other demand moved in the State Legislative Assembly. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The 36th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1975 made Sikkim a full-fledged State of the Indian Union. It included a new **Article 371-F** containing special provisions with respect to Sikkim. These are as follows:
○ The Sikkim Legislative Assembly is to consist of not less than 30 members. ○ **One seat is allotted to Sikkim in the Lok Sabha** and Sikkim forms one Parliamentary constituency. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Article 371-I** provides that the Goa Legislative Assembly is to **consist of not less than 30 members. So,** **pair III is correctly matched.** **So, all three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following events:
I. 	 Addition of the terms “Socialist” and “Secular” to the Preamble
II. 	 Manipur, Meghalaya and Tripura attaining statehood
III. 	Recognition of the Right to Education as a Fundamental Right
IV. 	 Inclusion of co-operative societies within the ambit of Fundamental Rights
Which of the following is the correct chronological sequence of the above events?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In **1972**, the political map of Northeast India underwent a major change. Thus, the **two Union Territories** **of Manipur and Tripura** and the sub-state of **Meghalaya got statehood** and the two Union Territories of Mizoram and Arunachal Pradesh (originally known as North-East Frontier Agency–NEFA) came into being. With this, the number of states of the Indian Union increased to 21 (**Manipur 19th, Tripura 20th** **and Meghalaya 21st**).
● The words **"socialist", "secular" and "integrity"** were inserted into the Preamble to the Constitution under the **42nd constitutional amendment** moved by the Indira Gandhi government in **1976.**
● **Article 21A** of the Constitution of India declares that the State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such a manner as the State may determine. This provision was added by the **86th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2002 to make** the Right to Education a Fundamental Right in part-III of the Constitution.
● **Part IX-B** of the Constitution of India contains the provisions with respect to the cooperative societies. The **97th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011** accorded **constitutional status and protection** to cooperative societies. It recognised the **right to form co-operative societies** as a **Fundamental Right** under **Article 19(1)(c)**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following offices are covered under both the Second and Third Schedule of the Constitution of India?
I. 	 The President of India
II. 	 The Comptroller and Auditor General of India
III. 	The Speaker and the Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha
IV. 	 The Judges of the High Courts
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **d**
**Explanation:**
● **Second Schedule of the Indian Constitution** contains provisions relating to the **emoluments,** **allowances, privileges** and so on of:
o   **The President of India** o   The Governors of States o   **The Speaker and the Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha** o   The Chairman and the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha o   The Speaker and the Deputy Speaker of the Legislative Assembly in the states o   The Chairman and the Deputy Chairman of the Legislative Council in the states o   The Judges of the Supreme Court o   **The Judges of the High Courts** o   **The Comptroller and Auditor-General of India**
● **Third Schedule** contains forms of **Oaths or Affirmations** for: o   The Union Ministers o   The candidates for election to the Parliament o   The Members of Parliament o   The Judges of the Supreme Court o   **The Comptroller and Auditor-General of India** o   The State Ministers o   The candidates for election to the State Legislature o   The Members of the State Legislature o   **The Judges of the High Courts**
● **The oath of the President is given under Article 60 of the Indian Constitution.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The office of the Governor of a State is an employment under the Central Government.
**Statement II:** The Governor of a State is appointed by the President by warrant under her hand and seal.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Articles 153 to 167 in Part VI of the Constitution of India** deal with the **State Executive.** The State Executive consists of the Governor, the Chief Minister, the Council of Ministers and the Advocate General of the State.
● The Governor is the chief executive of the State. The Governor also acts as an agent of the Central Government. Therefore, the office of the Governor has a dual role.
● The Governor is **neither directly elected by the people nor indirectly elected** by a specially constituted electoral college as is the case with the President. He is **appointed by the President by warrant under** **her hand and seal.** In a way, he is a nominee of the Central government. But, as held by the Supreme Court in 1979, the **office of the Governor of a State is not an employment under the Central** **government.** It is an independent constitutional office and is **not under the control of or subordinate** **to the Central government.** The mode of appointment does not determine whether an office amounts to employment under the Central government. **So, statement I is not correct and statement II is correct.** **So, Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Commission Established by the Amendment
I. - National Commission for Backward Classes
102nd Constitutional Amendment Act,
II. - National Commission for Scheduled Castes
90th Constitutional Amendment Act,
III. National Commission for Scheduled Tribes
87th Constitutional Amendment Act,
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC)** was initially constituted by the Central Government by the **National Commission for Backward Classes Act, 1993** and so far the Commission has been reconstituted **7 times up to 2016.** The National Commission for Backward Classes Act, 1993 (27 of 1993), has been repealed through the National Commission for Backward Classes (Repeal) Act, 2018. The present Commission has been accorded Constitutional Status and constituted through the **102nd** **Constitutional Amendment Act of 2018**, whereby **Article 338B** has been inserted, forming a Commission for the socially and educationally backward classes to be known as the National Commission for Backward Classes. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The **National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC)** is a constitutional body, established to safeguard the rights and interests of Scheduled Castes (SCs). In **1978**, the Government of India constituted a **non-statutory Commission for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes**. This body was later renamed the **National Commission for SCs and STs** in **1987**.
○ The **65th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1990** replaced this arrangement by establishing a **constitutional National Commission**, thereby elevating its status and authority. Subsequently, the **89th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003** bifurcated the Commission into two separate constitutional bodies, the **National Commission for Scheduled Castes** and the **National** **Commission for Scheduled Tribes**. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● The **National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (STs)** is a constitutional body in the sense that it is directly established by Article 338-A of the Constitution. To safeguard the interests of **Scheduled Tribes** **(STs)** more effectively, it was decided to establish a **separate National Commission for Scheduled** **Tribes** by bifurcating the existing combined Commission for SCs and STs. This was achieved through the **89th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003**, which **amended Article 338** and **inserted a new Article** **338A** into the Constitution. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText: Which one of the following appoints India’s Ambassadors and High Commissioners to foreign countries?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India, the **Ministry of External Affairs** is responsible for all aspects of **external relations.** Territorial divisions deal with bilateral political and economic work while functional divisions look after policy planning, multilateral organizations, regional groupings, legal matters, disarmament, protocol, consular, Indian Diaspora, press and publicity, **administration and other aspects.**
● However, the **formal appointment of Indian Ambassadors and High Commissioners** is made by the **President of India,** acting on the recommendations of the Prime Minister and the Council of Ministers. Most appointees belong to the Indian Foreign Service (IFS), though a small number of non-career diplomats may also be appointed. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● An **Ambassador** represents one **sovereign state in another foreign country**, whereas a **High** **Commissioner** represents a country in another state within the **Commonwealth.**
● An Ambassador heads an **Embassy**, while a High Commissioner heads a **High Commission.**
● Countries that are not members of the Commonwealth exchange Ambassadors, whereas Commonwealth countries appoint High Commissioners to one another.
● In terms of diplomatic functions and status, Ambassadors and High Commissioners perform broadly similar roles, representing their home country’s interests and engaging with the host government and society. The difference lies primarily in historical and institutional arrangements, not in the level of authority or commitment to local interests.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Supreme Courts of India and
the USA, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court
of India is limited to federal disputes, whereas
the Supreme Court of the USA includes cases
involving ambassadors and maritime matters.
II. 	 The appellate jurisdiction of the Supreme
Court of India extends to civil and criminal
cases, while the Supreme Court of the USA is
confined to constitutional cases only.
III. 	The powers of the Supreme Court of India can
be expanded by Parliament, whereas those of
the Supreme Court of the USA are restricted
to the Constitution.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Indian and American judiciaries** differ across several parameters, including the **judicial system**, **appellate jurisdiction**, **advisory jurisdiction**, **judicial discretion**, **scope of judicial review** and other institutional features.
● Under **Article 131 of the Constitution of India**, the Supreme Court of India is given exclusive jurisdiction in a dispute between the Union and a State or between one State and another, or between a group of States and others. The **original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India is confined to federal cases** whereas the American Supreme Court has original jurisdiction not only over federal cases but also cases relating to **naval forces, maritime activities, ambassadors**, etc. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Article 132 of the Constitution of India provides for an appellate jurisdiction to the Supreme Court from any judgement or final order of a **court in civil, criminal or other proceedings of a High Court;** if it involves a substantial question of law as to the interpretation of the Constitution. Whereas the appellate jurisdiction of the **Supreme Court of the USA is confined to constitutional cases only. So, statement** **II is correct.**
● In India, the **jurisdiction and powers of the Supreme Court** can be **expanded by Parliament** through legislation. In contrast, the **Supreme Court of the United States** exercises only those powers that are **explicitly granted by the Constitution**, with little scope for legislative expansion. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about the
reasonable restrictions of the Fundamental Rights
of the Civil Servants in India:
I. 	 They are forbidden to use their influence in an
election to any legislature or local authority.
II. 	 They enjoy the absolute right to form
associations or join associations already in
existence.
III. 	They are prohibited from making statements
that criticise government policy or actions.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **right of political activities** of the **public servants** in a democratic government is determined by the constitutional theory of government. Political neutrality of civil servants has been regarded as one of the cardinal conditions for the success of a democratic government.
● In India, the government servants, under the Central Civil Services (Conduct) Rules, 1964, are not free to indicate the manner in which they propose to vote or have voted. **They are forbidden** to canvas or **use** **their influence in an election to any legislature or local authority.** The government servants are not expected to attend election meetings organised by any political party except in their official capacity. They cannot stand for election to the Parliament or to the State Legislature. They are required to resign to contest elections. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Civil servants in India have the **right to form or join associations;** however, this **right is not absolute** and is regulated by the Central Civil Services (Conduct) Rules, 1964. The **Delhi High Court** in **Central** **PWD Engineers Association v. Union of India (2023)** has held that government employees cannot be denied the Fundamental Right to form associations under Article 19(1)(c), but they **must comply with** **prescribed rules,** including the requirement that such associations obtain government recognition to function lawfully. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Under the **Central Civil Services (Conduct) Rules, 1964,** a government servant cannot, **without prior** **government sanction, own, manage, edit, or participate in any newspaper, periodical, or electronic** **media.** It further **prohibits government servants from making statements that criticise government** **policy or actions**, or that may embarrass Centre–State or international relations, except for bona fide expressions by recognised associations or trade unions on service matters, or statements made in an official capacity. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements: In a democracy:
I. 	 the government should be elected by the people
II. 	 each adult citizen must have one vote
III. 	decisions are entirely based on constitutional supremacy
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Democracy** is a system of government in which laws, policies, leadership and major undertakings of a State or other polity are directly or indirectly decided by the people.
● The following are the features of a democracy: ○ The **final decision-making power** must rest with those **elected by the people. So, statement I is** **correct.** ○ A democracy must be based on a free and fair election where those currently in power have a fair chance of losing. ○ In a democracy, **each adult citizen must have one vote** and each vote must have one value. **So,** **statement II is correct.** ○ A democratic government rules within limits set by constitutional law and citizens’ rights and **not** **entirely based on constitutional supremacy**. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following goods and services:
I. 	 Personal life insurance
II. 	 UHT (Ultra-High Temperature) milk
III. 	Pre-packaged & labelled paneer
IV. 	 Bio-medical waste treatment services
V. 	 Essential learning materials like exercise books
How many of the above are zero-rated under Goods and Services Tax (GST) in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **GST (Goods and Services Tax)** is an indirect tax imposed on the supply of goods and services. It is a multi-stage, destination-oriented tax imposed on every value addition, replacing multiple indirect taxes, including VAT, excise duty, service taxes, etc. Goods and services are included under a single domestic indirect taxation law for the whole of India. In this regime, tax is charged at each point of sale.
● The **Goods and Services Tax (GST) reforms** introduced in **September 2025** represent a landmark step in **reshaping India’s taxation system to** **better serve the aspirations of its youth**.
● The GST rate on **all Individual Life Insurance and Individual Health Insurance policies,** including reinsurance of the same, has been reduced from **18% to zero. So, point I is correct.**
● **Daily food items** like UHT milk, roti, paratha, paneer, and packaged snacks are brought under **5% or Nil** **GST**, easing household expenses.  **So, points II and III are correct.**
● GST on services for the **treatment or disposal of biomedical waste** has been reduced from 12% to **5%.** **So, point IV is not correct.**
○ GST on essential medicines reduced from 12% to 5% or Nil, easing the financial burden on patients. Greater affordability for long-term treatment of chronic illnesses such as diabetes, hypertension, and cancer.
● GST on **pencils, sharpeners, crayons, exercise books, graph books, maps, and charts** has been reduced from **12% to Nil**. This reduction will make basic learning tools cheaper, especially for government schools, children and youth in rural areas. It reduces out-of-pocket education expenses for families, encourages higher school enrolment, and supports better learning outcomes. At the same time, it boosts demand for domestic stationery manufacturing, particularly among MSMEs. **So, point V is correct.** **So, only four of the above are zero-rated under Goods and Services Tax (GST) in India.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Trade (Exports, Imports, FDI, FII, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) trends in India from FY01 (2000-2001) to FY25 (2024-25), consider the following statements:
I. 	 India’s FDI inflows have increased by around 20 times.
II. 	 Singapore and Mauritius together accounted for nearly half of the total cumulative FDI inflows into India.
III. 	Maharashtra has been the single largest recipient of FDI equity inflows, accounting for about one-fourth of the total inflows.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)** stands as a key catalyst for India's economic growth, constituting a substantial non-debt financial reservoir for the nation's developmental endeavours. International corporations strategically invest in India, capitalizing on the country's unique investment incentives, including tax incentives and relatively competitive labour costs.
● India's FDI inflows have **increased ~20 times from FY01 to FY25**. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ According to the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)**, India's cumulative FDI inflow stood at US$ 1.12 trillion. between April 2000 and September 2025, mainly due to the government's efforts to improve the ease of doing business and ease of FDI norms. ○ From April 2000 to September 2025, **India's service sector** attracted the highest FDI equity inflow of 16%, amounting to Rs. 8,09,564 crore (US$ 123.94 billion), followed by the computer software and hardware industry at 16%, amounting
● India also had major FDI inflows during April 2000-September 2025, coming from **Singapore** with a total share of 24%, followed by **Mauritius** at 24%, the **USA** at 10%, the **Netherlands** at 7%, and **Japan** at 6%. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ Singapore, followed by the Netherlands, Mauritius, the USA and the UAE, together accounted for more than three-fourths of total inflows. Manufacturing and services, including communication, computer and business services were the top recipient sectors.
● The state that received the highest FDI equity inflow during April 2000-September 2025 was **Maharashtra** at 31%, followed by **Karnataka** at 21%, **Gujarat** at 15%, **Delhi** at 13%, and **Tamil Nadu** at 6%.  **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: GDP, Inflation, Unemployment
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is a major limitation of using the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) as a deflator for estimating Real GDP in India?

options.a: It does not adequately capture price changes in the services sector.
options.b: It only reflects the consumer price situation in the country.
options.c: It assigns relatively higher weight to food and beverages.
options.d: It does not include price changes in imported goods.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Wholesale Price Index** (WPI) reflects changes in the average prices of goods **at the wholesale level,** that is, commodities sold in bulk and traded between businesses or entities rather than goods bought by consumers.
● There are certain limitations in using WPI as a measure of inflation, as it does not include the **prices of** **services** and **does not reflect the country's consumer price situation.**
● WPI is released by the **Economic Advisor in the Ministry of Commerce and Industry.**
● The purpose of WPI is to inspect movement in prices of goods that reflect supply and demand in industry, construction and manufacturing. The index basket of WPI categorises commodities under three groups: **primary articles (22.6%), Manufactured products (64.2%), Fuel and Power (13.15%).**  The WPI includes **several items that are imported**, and its sensitivity to global commodity prices is often cited as a reason it can diverge from domestic retail inflation.
● In April 2017, the government revised the base year for WPI from 2004-05 to **2011-12.**
● WPI-based inflation is used by the government in preparation of fiscal, trade, and other economic policies. Business organisations, policymakers, accountants, and statisticians use WPI as an indexing tool to formulate price adjustment clauses. Rise in WPI indicates inflationary pressure in the economy, and vice versa. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Financial Markets
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements, with regard to Urban Cooperative Banks (UCBs) in India:
I. 	 These are registered under the Cooperative Societies Act of a State or the Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act, 2002.
II. 	 The Reserve Bank of India has extended the Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) framework to UCBs.
III. 	These are required to achieve a Priority Sector Lending (PSL) target of 40%.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Urban Cooperative Banks (UCBs) are financial institutions operating in **urban and semi-urban areas** of India. They primarily cater to the banking requirements of **small businesses, individuals, and local** **communities**.
● UCBs are **registered as cooperative societies** under the Co-operative Societies Acts of the respective **State Governments**. Those with a **multi-State presence** are registered under the **Multi-State Co-** **operative Societies Act**, administered by the Government of India.  **So, statement I is correct.**
○ UCBs are subject to dual regulation by the State Registrar of Cooperative Societies (for administrative matters) and the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) for banking functions under the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
● The RBI has **extended the Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) framework to UCBs** (effective April 1, 2025), replacing the earlier Supervisory Action Framework (SAF), to strengthen financial discipline based on capital adequacy, asset quality, and profitability parameters.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Priority Sector Lending** (PSL) target for Urban Cooperative Banks (UCBs) has been **revised** **downward to 60%, from the earlier 75%**, of their Adjusted Net Bank Credit (ANBC) or Credit Equivalent of Off-Balance Sheet Exposures (CEOBE), whichever is higher. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText: With reference to DIGIPIN, which one of the following statements is correct?

options.a: It is a 10-character alphanumeric digital code developed as a geo-coded digital address system by the Department of Posts.
options.b: It is a 6-digit unique banking identification number assigned by the Reserve Bank of India to digital payment users.
options.c: It is a consumer location index compiled by the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation.
options.d: It is a satellite-based navigation grid developed by ISRO to be used exclusively for defence logistics.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **DIGIPIN** is a digital addressing system introduced by the **Department of Posts (India Post)**.
● It is an **open-source, nationwide, geocoded addressing system** developed in collaboration with **IIT** **Hyderabad** and **NRSC–ISRO**.
● Unlike conventional postal PIN codes (which represent large areas), DIGIPIN assigns a **unique 10-** **character alphanumeric code** to a **precise 4m × 4m grid location**.
● The code is generated from **latitude and longitude coordinates**, making it a **coordinate-based** **geospatial reference system** rather than a descriptive address.
● It aims to: ○ Improve delivery efficiency (e-commerce, logistics), ○ Enhance emergency response services, ○ Reduce errors due to incomplete or unstructured addresses.
● DIGIPIN **does not replace existing postal addresses**; it acts as a complementary digital layer.
● The system supports **offline functionality**, and its programming logic is placed in the public domain.
● It does **not store personal data**, ensuring privacy protection. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Services
questions.questionText:
Recently retail inflation in India declined to a
multi-year low. Which of the following factors
primarily contributed to this decline?
I. 	 Moderation in food inflation
II. 	 Stable fuel prices
III. 	Significant decline in core inflation in housing
and services
IV. 	 Government supply-side interventions, such
as the release of buffer stocks
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The recent **sharp decline in retail inflation** to multi-year lows (e.g., around **0.3% in October 2025**) was driven **primarily by a disinflationary trend in food prices**, as food items that form a large share of the CPI basket saw sustained declines. Declining food prices were a key reason for the fall in headline inflation.  **So, point I is correct.**
● **Fuel prices helped contain inflation**, with broader food and fuel together accounting for over half of the CPI basket. Easing energy and food prices supported the disinflation process. **So, point II is correct.**
● Core inflation (which excludes food and fuel) **did not fall along with headline inflation**. In fact, it remained relatively stable or showed only modest changes and did not contribute to the decline in headline inflation. **So, point III is not correct.**
● Government supply-side interventions like **timely trade policies, buffer stock management and** **targeted interventions** helped manage food prices and reduce volatility, thus contributing to the fall in inflation. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following items:
I. 	 Patents
II. 	 Agricultural machinery
III. 	Finished goods ready for sale
IV. 	 Commercial paper
V. 	 Software licences
How many of the above are included in the
category of fixed capital?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Fixed capital** refers to the portion of a business’s total investment allocated to long-term assets such as factories, machinery, and vehicles. These assets have been used for many years and are not intended to be sold in the near term.
● In general, fixed capital can be categorised into two types, these are: ○ **Tangible Fixed Capital:** Tangible assets are a specific category of fixed capital that is physical; hence, these assets can be seen and touched. **It includes assets such as machinery, buildings,** **vehicles, and land, as they have a physical presence and a transactional monetary value. So,** **point II is correct.** ○ **Intangible Fixed Capital:** In contrast, intangible fixed capital refers to non-physical assets that provide long-term value to a business. Examples of intangible fixed capital include **patents,** **copyrights, trademarks, and software licenses**. While these assets lack a physical presence, they are crucial for enhancing the overall worth of a company. **So, points I and V are correct.**
● **Finished goods ready for sale** are part of working capital (inventory) as they are intended to be sold quickly for cash. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Commercial paper** is a short-term money-market instrument (debt), classified as a **liquid asset** rather than fixed capital. **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only three of the above are included in the category of fixed capital.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Monetary Policy
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements about the
Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR) auctions is
**not** correct?

options.a:
It is used by the RBI to absorb excess liquidity
from the banking system.
options.b:
Its rate is determined through competitive
bidding by banks.
options.c:
It increases the cash reserves of commercial
banks.
options.d:
It helps in fine-tuning short-term interest rates
in the money market.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Reserve Bank of India’s decision to conduct four Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR) auctions was viewed by bankers and analysts as a possible signal of an impending exit from unconventional monetary easing. Although the broader policy stance remained accommodative to support growth, the step indicated a gradual recalibration of liquidity conditions.
● **VRRR is a monetary policy tool** used for liquidity management. Through this instrument, banks park surplus funds with the RBI via an **auction-based mechanism** in which the interest rate is discovered through **competitive bidding** rather than being pre-fixed. By doing so, it **withdraws excess liquidity** from the banking system and **reduces banks’ lendable resources. Liquidity absorption helps stabilize** **short-term rates in the money market.**
○ VRRR specifically targets excess liquidity. **It does not change the statutory Cash Reserve Ratio** **(CRR) requirements.**
● In contrast, the Variable Rate Repo (VRR) injects short-term liquidity into the system. While both instruments operate through auctions and market-based rate discovery, they perform opposite functions. **VRR adds liquidity, whereas VRRR absorbs surplus funds.**
**Therefore, option (c) is the answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Buddhist monastic tradition,
the term ‘Pravrajya’ refers to the:

options.a:
Final stage of enlightenment attained by a
monk
options.b:
Ritual of worship performed by non-monastic
devotees at stupas
options.c: Council held for codifying the Buddhist canon
options.d:
First step of becoming a monk by leaving
household life

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In **Buddhist monastic tradition**, **Pravrajya (Pali: pabbajjā)** refers to the act of “going forth” from **household life into homelessness**, marking the **lower ordination** of a novice monk. It was the first formal step before **full ordination (Upasampada).**
● During the Pravrajya ceremony, the aspirant typically **shaved the head, wore ochre robes**, and accepted a **preceptor (teacher).** The novice then recited the Three Refuges: **Buddha, Dhamma, and Sangha**, and undertook ten basic vows.
● Pravrajya symbolises **renunciation** and entry into the **Sangha** as a **trainee monk.** Full membership and **higher monastic discipline** were achieved later through **Upasampada**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Which of the following Buddhist sites contain
the stone portrait that is considered to be the
only surviving image of the Ashoka and had the
inscription ‘Raya Asoko’ in Brahmi on it?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Kanagamahalli Buddhist site** is situated on the bank of the river Bhima near Kanaganahalli in **Kalaburagi** district **in Karnataka.**
● It has a Maha Stupa, which was referred to as **Adholoka Maha Chaitya** (the Great Stupa of the netherworlds) in the Ashokan inscriptions. The Kanagamahalli Buddhist Stupa was constructed using locally available limestone.
● It also contains the **stone-portrait of Emperor Ashoka**, surrounded by his queens and female attendants. While the Stupa is believed to be one of the largest of its time, the stone-portrait is considered to be the **only surviving image** of the Mauryan Emperor which had the **inscription ‘Raya Asoko’ in Brahmi on** **it.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Sangam Age
questions.questionText:
Consider the following texts:
I. 	 Silappadikaram
II. 	 Civaka Cintamani
III. 	Kalakacharya Katha
IV. 	 Visuddhimagga
V. 	 Parishishtaparvan
How many of the above are associated with
Jainism?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Jain literature** constitutes an extensive and ancient corpus of religious, philosophical, and secular writings that preserve the doctrines and traditions of the Tirthankaras. Composed mainly in **Prakrit**, **particularly Ardhamagadhi**, it forms the foundational textual heritage of Jainism.
● **Silappadikaram** is one of the **Five Great Epics of Tamil Literature**, composed in the Sangam age between the **5th and 6th centuries CE.** It is composed by **Ilango Adigal** and reflects **Jain philosophical** **themes** and makes references to Jain ascetics and temples. It is a foundational work that blends **poetry,** **music, and drama** to narrate a tragic story of love, injustice, and revenge, while offering a detailed, panoramic view of **ancient Tamil society.** **So, point I is correct.**
● **Civaka Chintamani** is also one of the five **major Tamil epics.** The epic was written in the **early 10th** **century** by **Tiruttakkatvar**, a Madurai-based **Jain monk.** It tells the narrative of a prince who is the **ideal** **master of all arts, a warrior, and a lover** with many wives. It is also known as the Mana Nool. **So, point** **II is correct.**
● The **Kalakacharya Katha** is a non-canonical **Jain legend** from the **Shvetambara tradition** that recounts the life, adventures, and magical feats of the Jain monk Kalakacharya. Dating back to roughly the **1st** **century BCE**, it tells the story of how the monk sought the help of **foreign Scythian (Saka) rulers** to overthrow a wicked king, Gardabhilla of Ujjain, who had abducted his sister. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **Visuddhimagga** is the great treatise on **Theravāda Buddhist doctrine** and meditation, composed by the renowned Indian **scholar-monk Buddhaghosa** in the **5th century CE** in Sri Lanka. It is considered the most authoritative **non-canonical text** in the Theravāda tradition, acting as a **comprehensive,** **systematic manual** that condenses the vast teachings of the **Pali Canon (Tipitaka)** into a practical, stepby-step guide to achieving **liberation (Nirvana).** **So, point IV is not correct.**
● The **Parishishtaparvan,** also known as the **Sthaviravalicharitra,** is a **12th-century** Sanskrit mahakavya by **Hemachandra** that details the histories of the **earliest Jain teachers.** It acts as an **appendix or a** **supplement** to his larger work, the **Trishashti Shalaka Purusha Caritra** (The Lives of the Sixty-three Illustrious People). **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Somnath Temple, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is revered as the first (Aadi Jyotirlinga) of the twelve holy Jyotirlingas.
II. 	 Its detailed account was provided by the Persian scholar Al-Biruni.
III. 	It was attacked and plundered by the Mughal ruler Babur in the 16th century.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Somnath Temple**, located in **Prabhas Patan, Gujarat**, is **one of the 12 sacred Jyotirlinga shrines** of Shiva, known for its **historical, religious, and architectural** significance. It has been **destroyed and** **rebuilt multiple times**, with the current structure, built in 1951.
● Somnath Temple is revered as the **first (Aadi Jyotirlinga)** of the twelve holy Jyotirlingas, where **Lord** **Shiva** appeared as a **fiery column of light.** It is considered the place where the **Moon God (Som)** worshipped Shiva to free himself from a curse. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The 11th-century **Persian scholar Al-Biruni** provided a **detailed account** of the Somnath Temple in his famous work, **Kitab-ul-Hind (or Indica).** He described the temple’s immense **grandeur**, its **location** on the coast, and its **significance** as a pilgrimage site, noting it was maintained by **revenue from thousands** **of villages. So, statement II is correct.**
● The shrine was notably attacked and plundered by **Mahmud of Ghazni** in 1024–1025 CE. In subsequent centuries, it faced further destruction during the rule of the Delhi Sultanate, including under **Alauddin** **Khalji,** and later during the reign of the Mughal emperor **Aurangzeb.** There is **no reliable historical** **evidence** linking **Babur** to its demolition. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● The **post-Independence reconstruction** was initiated in **1947** by **Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel,** who laid the foundation. **President Rajendra Prasad** attended the formal **inauguration** in 1951. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Prime Minister of India addressed the Somnath Swabhiman Parv at Somnath in Gujarat.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Swadeshi Movement
II. 	 Hindu Mahasabha
III. 	All India Trade Union Congress
IV. 	 Quit India Movement Lala Lajpat Rai was associated with how many of the above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Swadeshi Movement** was launched in **1905** against the **partition of Bengal. This** movement promoted **indigenous goods** and national education. **Lala Lajpat Rai, alongside Tilak and Bipin** **Chandra Pal (the Lal-Bal-Pal trio)**, spearheaded the movement outside Bengal, specifically mobilizing the masses in Punjab. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Hindu Mahasabha**, a politico-religious organization, was formed by **Madan Mohan Malviya** in the year **1915** to protect the **rights of the Hindu community** in British India. **Lala Lajpat Rai** was a **key figure** who helped build the organization's **foundational philosophy**, actively promoted its agenda of Hindu unity, and served as a prominent leader of the organization. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC)** was founded in **1920** in **Bombay**, with **Lala Lajpat Rai** as its **first president.** It aimed to organise industrial labour, secure **workers’ rights**, improve **wages**, and address working conditions in British India. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **Quit India Movement** was launched by Mahatma Gandhi on **8 August 1942** through the All India Congress Committee in **Bombay**. **Lala Lajpat Rai had died** in **1928** after injuries from a police lathi charge, so he had **no direct role in it.** **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Why did the Indian National Congress accept the Swarajists’ proposal of entering the legislative councils in the mid-1920s?
I. 	 To maintain political momentum after the suspension of the Non-Cooperation Movement
II. 	 To prevent pro-government elements from dominating the legislative councils
III. 	To secure purna swaraj through constitutional negotiations with the British Government
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● After the **suspension** of the **Non-Cooperation Movement** in **1922** following the **Chauri Chaura** **incident**, Congress faced **organisational stagnation** and internal division between **No-Changers** and **Pro-Changers.** The Swarajists, led by **C.R. Das** and **Motilal Nehru**, advocated council entry to continue anti-colonial resistance.
● The **abrupt withdrawal** of the Non-Cooperation Movement created **political inertia.** Council entry was seen as a means to **sustain nationalist enthusiasm** and prevent demoralisation. **The Swaraj Party** **(formed in 1923)** sought to keep the struggle active within constitutional forums. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The Swarajists argued that continued boycott would allow **loyalists and pro-government moderates** to **dominate councils** under the Government of India Act, 1919. By entering legislatures, they aimed to **obstruct budgets**, **expose dyarchy’s weaknesses,** and challenge colonial governance from within. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● During the mid-1920s, the primary goal of the Swarajists was to **achieve Swaraj (Self-rule) or Dominion** **Status** within the British Empire, often through a strategy of "uniform, continuous, and consistent obstruction" to make the councils unworkable. The goal of **Purna Swaraj** (Complete Independence) was not officially adopted by the Indian National Congress until the **Lahore Session in December 1929.** **So,** **statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Proposal Main Provision
I. - Deepavali Declaration (1929)
Dominion Status for India in the future
II. - August Offer (1940) Expansion of the Viceroy’s Executive Council
III. - Cripps Mission (1942)
Constituent Assembly after World War II
IV. - Cabinet Mission Plan (1946)
Immediate grant of complete independence
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Deepavali Declaration (1929)**, made by **Viceroy Lord Irwin**, promised **Dominion Status for India** in the future and proposed a **Round Table Conference**. It did not immediately grant **self-government** but acknowledged Dominion Status as the goal. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The **August Offer (1940)** proposed **expansion of the Viceroy’s Executive Council,** inclusion of **more** **Indians** in governance, and recognition of **Indians’ right to frame a constitution** after the war. **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.**
● The **Cripps Mission (1942)** proposed establishing an Indian Union with **Dominion** status after World War II, a **Constituent Assembly** elected **partly** by provincial assemblies and **nominated** by princely states, and, crucially, allowed provinces to **opt out of the new constitution.** This provision was heavily opposed by Congress, leading to the mission's failure. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● The **Cabinet Mission Plan (1946)** proposed a **federal structure** with groupings of provinces and a **Constituent Assembly.** Although it recognized the Indian right to cede from the Commonwealth, which was a significant step toward full sovereignty, It **did not grant immediate complete independence. So,** **pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Communist Party of India (CPI) initially decided to stay away from the Quit India Movement launched in August 1942.
**Statement II:** The CPI was in support of the demand for the creation of Pakistan on the basis of the right of nationalities to self-determination.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement-I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II is **not** the correct explanation for Statement-I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Communist Party of India (CPI) stayed away** from the **Quit India Movement** initially, but after **Germany invaded the Soviet Union** in 1941, the Communist Party **shifted its line** and supported the **British war effort** against fascism. It characterised World War II as a **People’s War and** opposed mass disruption of the **anti-Axis struggle. So, statement I is correct.**
● During the 1940s, the CPI advanced the **theory of nationalities** and **supported** the **Muslim League’s** **demand for Pakistan** on the principle of **self-determination,** viewing India as a **multinational entity.** **So, statement II is correct.**
● However, the CPI’s support for Pakistan was **not the reason for its opposition to the Quit India** **Movement.** The latter was primarily due to its pro-Allied wartime stance after 1941. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct, but Statement II is** **not** **the correct explanation for** **Statement I.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText: Which one of the following was the feature of the Ahmedabad Mill Strike in 1918:

options.a: Active all-India participation of mill workers across India.
options.b: Mahatma Gandhi undertook his first fast unto death in India during this movement.
options.c: Raj Kumar Shukla persuaded Mahatma Gandhi to intervene in the worker’s cause.
options.d: The workers established virtual parallel governments across states.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In March 1918, Gandhi intervened in a dispute between **cotton mill owners of Ahmedabad** and the **workers** over the issue of **discontinuation of the plague bonus**. The mill owners wanted to withdraw the bonus. The workers were demanding a rise of 50 per cent in their wages. The mill owners were ready to give only a 20 per cent wage hike. It led to the workers going on strike.
● **Gandhi was invited by Anusuya Behn** to intervene and help resolve the impasse between the workers and the employers. Gandhi was respected by the mill owners and workers. **Gandhi asked the workers to** **go on a strike and demand a 35 per cent increase in wages instead of 50 per cent.**
● Gandhi advised the workers to remain non-violent while on strike. When negotiations with mill owners did not progress, **Gandhi undertook his first fast unto death in India to strengthen the workers’** **resolve.** The fast also had the **effect of putting pressure on the mill owners** who finally agreed to **submit** **the issue to a tribunal**. The strike was withdrawn. In the end, the tribunal awarded the workers a 35 per cent wage hike.
● There was no all-India participation of mill workers across India in the strike and no parallel governments were set up in the state.
● Raj Kumar Shukla persuaded Mahatma Gandhi to intervene during the Champaran Satyagraha. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following events in the history of the Indian National Movement:
I. 	 Annulment of the Partition of Bengal
II. 	 Komagata Maru Incident
III. 	Jallianwala Bagh Massacre
IV. 	 Lucknow Pact What is the correct chronological order of the above events, starting from the earliest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Annulment of the Partition of Bengal** occurred in **1911** during the **Delhi Durbar** of King George
V. The 1905 partition by **Lord Curzon** had triggered the **Swadeshi Movement** and widespread nationalist protest. **In 1911, the British reversed** the partition and shifted the capital from **Calcutta to Delhi.**
● The **Komagata Maru Incident** took place in **1914**, when a ship carrying Indian migrants, mostly Sikhs **led by Gurdit Singh**, was denied entry into Canada under discriminatory immigration laws. On returning to **Calcutta, British police** fired upon passengers at **Budge Budge**, killing several passengers.
● The **Lucknow Pact** was signed in **1916** between the **Indian National Congress** and the **All-India** **Muslim League.** It marked a **rare phase of Hindu–Muslim unity** and accepted **separate electorates** for Muslims while demanding constitutional **reforms from the British.**
● The **Jallianwala Bagh Massacre** occurred on **13 April 1919** in **Amritsar**, when **General Dyer** ordered troops to fire on an unarmed gathering protesting **the Rowlatt Act,** killing hundreds and profoundly **radicalising the national movement.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Event Governor-General/ Viceroy in Office
I. - Revolt of 1857 Lord Canning
II. - Chauri Chaura incident Lord Reading
III. - Bombing in the Central Legislative Assembly
Lord Irwin
IV. - Chittagong Armoury Raid Lord Willingdon
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Revolt of 1857** occurred during the tenure of **Lord Canning (1856–1862)**, who was the **last** **Governor-General** of the East India Company and later became the first Viceroy under the Crown, by the Government of India Act, 1858. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The **Chauri Chaura Incident (1922)** took place during the viceroyalty of **Lord Reading (1921–1926).** The violent clash led Mahatma Gandhi to **suspend** the **Non-Cooperation Movement,** marking a major strategic turning point in the national movement. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Central Legislative Assembly Bombing (1929)** was carried out by **Bhagat Singh** and **B.K. Dutt** during the tenure of **Lord Irwin (1926–1931).** The bombing aimed to protest repressive laws like the **Public Safety Bill. So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● The **Chittagong Armoury Raid (1930),** led by **Surya Sen**, occurred during **Lord Irwin’s tenure,** **not** **Lord Willingdon's** (who assumed office later in 1931). **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
With reference to Peripheral Immune Tolerance,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 It prevents immune cells from attacking the
body’s own tissues.
II. 	 It operates only in the lymph nodes and
thymus.
III. 	It plays a crucial role in preventing food
allergies and intestinal inflammation.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Peripheral immune tolerance** is a critical, **post-maturation mechanism** of the **adaptive immune** **system** that **prevents** self-reactive **T and B immune cells** from **attacking the body's own tissues** after they leave primary lymphoid organs. It serves as a backup to central tolerance, utilizing **regulatory T** **cells (Tregs)**, clonal anergy, and deletion to maintain self-tolerance and **immune homeostasis. So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Peripheral tolerance **does not operate only in the lymph nodes and thymus.** The thymus is the site of central tolerance. Peripheral tolerance primarily functions in **secondary lymphoid organs** (like lymph nodes and spleen) and in **peripheral tissues** such as the gut, skin, and lungs through regulatory T cells, **clonal anergy, and apoptosis. So, statement II is not correct.**
● Peripheral immune tolerance is essential in **maintaining tolerance** to **harmless antigens** such as food proteins and **commensal gut microbiota.** Regulatory T cells in intestinal tissues **suppress excessive** **immune responses**, preventing food allergies, inflammatory bowel disease, and **chronic intestinal** **inflammation. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The 2025 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to Mary E. Brunkow, Fred Ramsdell, and Shimon Sakaguchi for their discoveries regarding peripheral immune tolerance.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Information and Communication Technology, Robotics, AI
questions.questionText:
With reference to the fibre optic cable, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 It transmits signals in the form of light pulses
through a glass or plastic core.
II. 	 It works on the principle of total internal
reflection.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Fibre optic cables** are **communication media** that transmit data as **light signals** through thin strands of glass or plastic. They enable **high-speed, low-loss transmission** over long distances and are widely used in telecommunications, **medical imaging,** and **defence applications.**
● Fibre optic cables transmit information as **pulses of light** generated by **lasers or LEDs.** These pulses travel through a **transparent core** made of **silica glass or plastic,** carrying digital data over long distances with **minimal signal degradation. So, statement I is correct.**
● The working principle of fibre optics is **total internal reflection.** Light entering the denser core **strikes** **the boundary** with the lower refractive index cladding at an angle **greater than the critical angle,** causing repeated internal reflection and forward propagation. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Fibre-optic drones have taken a more central role on the battlefields in the ongoing Russia-Ukraine war.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Mounjaro, Wegovy and Ozempic, sometimes seen
in the news, are:

options.a:
Gene-editing technologies developed for
metabolic diseases
options.b:
Monoclonal antibody therapies used in the
treatment of autoimmune disorders
options.c:
MRNA-based vaccines used for the prevention
of viral infections
options.d:
Medications used for the management of
obesity and type 2 diabetes

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mounjaro (tirzepatide)**, **Wegovy (semaglutide 2.4 mg)**, and **Ozempic (semaglutide)** are **GLP-1** **receptor** agonist–based **drugs** used for managing **type 2 diabetes** and **obesity**. GLP-1 (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1) is a gut hormone that regulates blood sugar, slows **gastric emptying, and reduces appetite.**
● **Wegovy and Ozempic** are developed by Novo Nordisk, while Mounjaro is produced by Eli Lilly. These drugs promote **significant weight loss** and improved glycaemic control. Tirzepatide also acts on GIP receptors, enhancing **metabolic effects.**
● Their rising demand has triggered **intense global competition**, particularly in **India**, where patent expiries of semaglutide are expected to enable **large-scale generic manufacturing.** The global anti-obesity drug market is projected to expand rapidly, with India emerging as both a **major consumer market** and a manufacturing hub under the **Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India is rapidly transforming into a crucial focal point for the global weight management industry.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Bacteria can generally infect which of the following?
I. 	 Virus
II. 	 Fungi
III. 	Algae
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Bacteria** are **microscopic**, **single-celled prokaryotic** organisms that live in nearly **every environment** on Earth, from soil and water to the human body. They are defined by **lacking a membrane-bound** **nucleus** and possessing a **rigid cell wall.**
● Viruses infect bacteria. These specific types of viruses are called bacteriophages or "phages". While bacteria do not "infect" viruses in the traditional sense, they have a complex and sometimes cooperative relationship. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Certain bacteria infect fungi**; these are known as **endofungal** or **mycoparasitic** bacteria. For example, **Burkholderia** species live inside **fungal cells** and influence fungal growth, **toxin production**, and **pathogenicity** in plant-fungal interactions. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Bacteria can infect algae**, particularly in aquatic **ecosystems**. Some bacterial species act as **algicidal** **pathogens**, invading algal cells and releasing **toxins or enzymes** that lyse them, influencing **algal bloom** **dynamics** and marine nutrient cycles. **So, point III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Stem cells possess the ability to self-renew and differentiate into specialised cell types.
II. 	 Embryonic stem cells are less versatile in differentiation than adult stem cells.
III. 	An autologous stem cell transplant uses healthy stem cells derived from the same individual.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Stem cells** are **undifferentiated cells** capable of **self-renewal and differentiation** into specialised cell types. They form the basis of **regenerative medicine** and are regulated in India under the **National** **Guidelines for Stem Cell Research.**
● Stem cells can divide **repeatedly (self-renewal)** while retaining their **undifferentiated** state. They can also differentiate into **specialised cells** such as neurons, muscle cells, or blood cells, making them crucial for **tissue repair** and **regenerative therapies**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Embryonic stem cells are **pluripotent**, meaning they can **differentiate into almost all cell types of the** **body.** **Adult stem cells** are usually **multipotent or** **unipotent**, restricted to specific tissues; therefore, embryonic stem cells are **more versatile. So, statement II is not correct.**
● An **autologous stem cell transplant** involves harvesting stem cells from a **patient’s own body,** commonly **bone** **marrow** or **peripheral blood**, before high-dose **chemotherapy or radiation, and** **reintroducing** them to restore blood-forming capacity and reduce immune rejection risks. **So,** **statement** **III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently,  the Supreme Court ruled that stem cell therapy cannot be offered as a clinical service for Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) outside approved clinical trials and directed the Government to create a dedicated regulatory authority for stem cell research.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
